By contrast, Los Angeles, New York, and Portland were the cities steadily remaining in the  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime throughout the study period. Also, we observed the sudden expansions of  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated conditions at the urban cores of Houston (in 2014) and Detroit (in 2017). In general, most cities in the eastern U.S., except Pittsburgh, tended to stay in the same regime to which they were in 2011 or even came back to the  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime compared to the cities in the western U.S. Many recent studies have reported the changes in ozone chemical regime over the major U.S. metropolitan areas toward  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited regime in general, but with a narrow range in the reported year when the regime change occurred, and cities still remained in  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited condition, which is related to the dataset, study period, and methodology of each study; Chang et al. (2016) showed that ozone formation in Boston, Pittsburgh, Philadelphia, and Washington has changed to  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited or transition regimes in 2007-2014, while New York City mostly stayed in the transition regime (2007-2009) and VOC-limited regime (2007-2009 and 2014). Jin et al. (2020) addressed that  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated condition is occurring only in Los Angeles, Chicago, and New York City by 2013-2016, and Koplitz et al. (2021) showed regime change toward  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited condition over most of the U.S., and a few cities such as New York City, Chicago, Minneapolis, San Francisco, and Los Angeles are still remained in  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime as in 2016.

In this study, we examined changes in the ozone chemical regime over the CONUS in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, years in which U.S. NEI data were based. To combine the benefits of both satellite retrievals and CTMs, we employed an inverse modeling technique with OMI tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column retrievals to estimate  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions. To avoid misalignment of the emission adjustment, we applied dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions and lightning-induced nitric oxide emissions, resulting in better representation of  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{O}_3$  concentration in the upper atmosphere, confirmed by comparisons with measurements from the DISCOVER-AQ campaigns and two ozonesonde sites.

Compared to OMI observations, the model with the top-down estimated  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions showed remarkably better performance for simulating tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO columns and alleviated the nationwide underestimation of the  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  column and the overestimation of the HCHO column appearing over the southeastern U. S. We then used the CMAQ model to determine the threshold values of the ozone production regime, which closely represented the ozone chemical conditions of this study:  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated (HCHO/  $\mathrm{NO}_2 < 1.6$ ), a transition  $(1.6 < \mathrm{HCHO} / \mathrm{NO}_2 < 2.6)$ , and  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited (HCHO/  $\mathrm{NO}_2 > 2.6$ ), which is slightly distinct from other studies by Chang et al. (2016), Choi et al. (2012), Duncan et al. (2010) and Jin et al. (2017). This is because the threshold value of the HCHO/  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  ratio represents the unique ozone chemistry of the CTM depending upon several influencing factors such as study domain, chemical mechanism, and meteorological condition; therefore, the applicability of the threshold value in other

Fig. 9. Changes in the ozone production regime over the 5 boxes denoted in Fig. 8.

To investigate changes in the ozone  $(\mathrm{O}_3)$  chemical production regime over the contiguous United States (CONUS) with accurate knowledge of concentrations of its precursors, we applied an inverse modeling technique with Ozone Monitoring Instrument (OMI) tropospheric nitrogen dioxide  $(\mathrm{NO}_2)$  and total formaldehyde (HCHO) retrieval products in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, years in which United States National Emission Inventory were based. The inclusion of dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions and lightning-induced nitric oxide emissions significantly account for the contribution of background sources in the free troposphere. Satellite-constrained nitrogen oxide  $(\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}})$  and non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs) emissions mitigate the discrepancy between satellite and modeled columns: the inversion suggested 2.33-2.84 (1.07-1.34) times higher  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  over the CONUS (over urban regions) and 0.28-0.81 times fewer NMVOCs emissions over the southeastern United States. The model-derived HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio shows gradual spatial changes in the  $\mathrm{O}_3$  production regime near urban cores relative to previously defined threshold values representing  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and VOC sensitive conditions. We also found apparent shifts from the  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime to the transition regime (or the transition regime to the  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ -limited regime) over the major cities in the western United States. In contrast, rural areas, especially in the east-southeastern United States, exhibit a decreased HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio by  $-1.30 \pm 1.71$  with a reduction in HCHO column primarily driven by meteorology, becoming sensitive to VOC emissions. Results show that incorporating satellite observations into numerical modeling could help policymakers implement appropriate emission control policies for  $\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution.

Figs. 5 and 6 display the spatial distribution of averaged tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column densities simulated with prior and posterior emissions corresponding to the OMI observations (shown in Fig. 2) and their differences with respect to the column with prior emissions. For both the  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns, the modeled columns with posterior emissions more closely agreed with those from the OMI observations. In most areas, the modeled tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  column, compared to that of the OMI observations, exhibited an underestimation throughout the study period (Figs. 2 and 5), but using posterior emission (shown in Fig. S5), we observed a large reduction in bias, increasing the  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  column by as much as  $21.55 \pm 28.76\%$  nationwide. The adjustment ratio between the prior and posterior  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions was slightly high in 2017  $(2.84 \pm 1.99)$  than in 2011  $(2.33 \pm 1.83)$  and 2014  $(2.62 \pm 2.19)$  over the CONUS (Fig. S8), predominantly in the central and western

threshold values by using the CMAQ model with posterior emission inventory based on the year 2011. Fig. 7 (a) represents the difference of averaged surface  $\mathrm{O_3}$  concentrations between the baseline CMAQ model and the CMAQ with perturbed emission (20% reductions in  $\mathrm{NO_x}$  and NMVOCs emissions) along with the corresponding modeled HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio for the revisit time of the OMI satellite (13:00-14:00 LST) since we adjusted emissions of ozone precursors based on OMI column retrievals. In addition, the revisit time of the OMI satellite is beneficial to analyze the ozone chemical regime when the ozone production is maximum in a day in conjunction with the smallest solar zenith angle and most profound boundary layer height (Jin et al., 2017). To clearly examine the difference of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  with perturbed emission, we considered model grids over polluted regions where the modeled tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  column is greater than  $1\times 10^{15}$  molecules  $\mathrm{cm}^{-2}$  (Boersma et al., 2016; Choi et al., 2012; Schaub et al., 2006). As a response to  $\mathrm{NO_x}$  emission reduction, surface  $\mathrm{O_3}$  concentrations generally increased proportionally to the HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio. The increase was dominant for a high HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio. The change in surface ozone concentrations became moderate for the case of NMVOCs emission reduction (red color); still, noticeable changes in ozone concentration were found for a low HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio.

An enhancement of model performance in the upper troposphere was also found by comparison with ozonesonde measurements, displayed in Fig. S4. content.title: Fig. 6. Six-year trends of the contributions from different sources to daytime OVOC concentrations.
content.summary: The figure displays six-year trends in contributions from various sources to daytime OVOC concentrations in Shenzhen during dry and wet seasons.
content.insight: The decreasing trend in primary anthropogenic sources indicates effective VOC control measures, while secondary sources show less variability, highlighting the complexity of managing OVOC levels.

metadata.title: Fig. 6. Six-year trends of the contributions from different sources to daytime OVOC concentrations.
references: Fig. 6 shows the trends in the relative contributions to OVOCs from 2014 to 2019 during the dry and wet seasons. In general, during the six years from 2014 to 2019, primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to OVOCs during Shenzhen's dry season, but the proportion exhibited a decreasing trend year over year, and the proportion during the six-year period decreased by approximately  $15\%$  overall. This continuous decreasing trend indicates that the VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas, where most of the industry and traffic of Shenzhen is located, had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. The OVOC source compositions during the wet season, when clean marine air masses from the south dominated the sampling site, did not exhibit a clear year-by-year trend. The contributions of secondary anthropogenic sources to OVOC concentrations (except that of methanol) during the wet season were significantly higher (approximately 1.3-1.6 times greater) than those during the dry season, indicating that secondary transformation of NMHCs during the wet season contributed greatly to OVOC concentrations. Biogenic sources reasonably contributed a larger proportion of OVOC concentrations during the wet season, whereas biomass burning contributed a larger fraction of acetaldehyde during the dry season.

Oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) are important precursors and intermediate products of atmospheric photochemical reactions, which can promote the formation of secondary pollutants such as ozone  $(\mathrm{O}_3)$  and secondary organic aerosol (SOA). However, there have been few studies on the sources of and long-term variation in ambient OVOCs. This study combined sensitive, near real-time measurements of VOCs by proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) with an improved photochemical age parameterization method to quantify daytime sources of OVOCs in an urban atmosphere in China from 2014 to 2019, permitting the observation of the impacts of emission control strategies that were implemented during this period. Temporal variation in six key OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), formic acid, and acetic acid) were observed. The sum of concentrations of OVOCs was averagely  $13\%$  higher during the dry season (November to April), when winds transported polluted air masses to Shenzhen from the continent, than during the wet season, and peak diurnal levels occurred during the daytime year-round due to photochemical production and higher daytime anthropogenic emissions. The average dry season concentration of OVOCs declined from a peak of 30.3 ppb in 2015 to 18.7 ppb in 2019. The results of source apportionment showed that primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to methanol, MEK, and acetic acid  $(32 - 51\%)$ ; the dominant sources of acetaldehyde and formic acid were both primary and secondary anthropogenic sources; and biomass burning contributed a small fraction  $(5 - 11\%)$  to the six OVOCs. From 2014 to 2019, contributions from primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs decreased significantly by  $50 - 60\%$  due to intensive pollution control measures in Shenzhen, whereas pollution control measures had no observable impact on secondary OVOCs, indicating their formation was not limited by availability of their primary VOC precursors.

As presented in Fig. 1, Shenzhen is located in the PRD of Guangdong Province, China. The sampling site for this study was located on the campus of the Shenzhen Graduate School of Peking University (PKUSZ,  $22.60^{\circ}\mathrm{N}$ ,  $113.97^{\circ}\mathrm{E}$ ) in western Shenzhen, which is predominantly surrounded by green areas, teaching buildings and apartments. No evident anthropogenic emission sources of air pollutants surrounded the sampling site, except a highway approximately  $100\mathrm{m}$  away. Previous studies have shown that the concentrations of air pollutants such as  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and VOCs at this site were approximately equal to their average concentrations in the entire PRD region (Louie et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2018). Therefore, this site was considered suitable for long-term observation of trends in air pollutants in the PRD region. Table S1 summarizes the effective days of VOCs observed in each month from 2014 to 2019, with 1169 days in total over the six years of sampling. During the six-year sampling period, the loss of data was mainly due to instrument failure and bad weather, especially in the wet season due to frequent rainfall, resulting in a higher rate of missing data during

Shenzhen belongs to the subtropical marine monsoon climate zone. Marked differences in the four seasons were not evident, and the observation time each year was slightly different; therefore, this study divided the entire year into a dry season and a wet season according to the monthly temperature and precipitation. As presented in Figure S1, the period from May to October, with higher temperatures and abundant precipitation, was defined as the wet season, and the period from November to April, with less precipitation, was defined as the dry season. The meteorological data are from the Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality (Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). In addition to the differences in temperature and precipitation, the sources of air masses in Shenzhen during the wet and dry seasons were also very different. Taking January and July 2018 as an example, the 48-h backward trajectories at  $100\mathrm{m}$  above ground level in Shenzhen  $(113.9^{\circ}\mathrm{E}, 22.6^{\circ}\mathrm{N})$  were calculated beginning at 12:00 UTC using the Hybrid Single Particle Lagrangian Integrated Trajectory (HYSPLIT) model (Draxler and Rolph, 2003). The results show that the origin of the air mass passing through Shenzhen in different seasons was very different: during the dry season, the air mass mainly came from the northern mainland, and during the wet season, it mainly came from the South China Sea, which has also been explained in detail in our previous studies (Zou et al., 2017). In addition, referencing existing studies of air pollution in the Pearl River Delta, many scholars have adopted a method of dividing the wet and dry seasons (Cui et al., 2015). Figure S2 shows the changes in major meteorological parameters during the six-year observation period in Shenzhen. Analysis of major meteorological parameters in Shenzhen (Fig S2) did not indicate significant interannual variability in temperature, rainfall, humidity or wind conditions that could have substantially influenced the long-term variation in atmospheric pollutants observed in this study.

It should be emphasized that because the sampling data of this study span six years, in addition to the above assumptions, one more assumption should be added: (4) during the sampling period, there was no significant change in the ratio of OVOC anthropogenic emissions to tracers (benzene) in Shenzhen. The emission reduction strategy of Shenzhen is to reduce emission sources of pollutants as a whole, such as replacing gasoline vehicles with electric vehicles and closing down or moving VOC-emitting enterprises to other areas. These measures have reduced emissions of VOCs, but the emission ratio among specific VOC species is relatively stable. Toluene/benzene (T/B) was considered a good indicator to judge the contribution of motor vehicle sources and solvent use to VOC emissions (Nelson and Quigley, 1984; Wang et al., 2002; Araizaga et al., 2013). Figure S5 shows the changes in the concentrations of benzene and toluene and T/B from 2014 to 2019. The concentrations of benzene and toluene in the six years showed a downward trend, while T/B remained at approximately 5, which also indicated

be due to the dilution caused by the uplift of the planetary boundary layer (PBL). However, the six OVOCs exhibited a significant upward trend during the daytime. These results suggested that photochemical production and/or daytime anthropogenic activities related to OVC emissions were notable and could overwhelm photochemical loss. The diurnal variation trend of acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O_3}$ , exhibiting a greater difference in concentration between day and night during the wet season, indicating that the majority of acetaldehyde originated from daytime secondary generation (Chang et al., 2019). The daytime splits of the diurnal curves of methanol, MEK, and acetic acid, similar to benzene, implied that they could have relatively larger contributions from primary emissions sources, such as motor vehicle emissions. (Guo et al., 2011, Zheng et al., 2013). The diurnal variations in VOCs in our previous two individual campaigns, which were carried out during the dry season (Huang et al., 2019, 2020), were almost the same as those in the dry season in this study.

The parameterization method based on photochemical aging described in section 2.4 was used to analyze the sources of OVOCs during the daytime (8:00-20:00). Referencing the source analysis results for the OVOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019 (in Table 2), the measured concentrations of six OVOC species monitored by PTR-MS were in good agreement with the simulated concentrations, and the measured values of each OVOC species were highly correlated with the simulated values (R was between 0.71 and 0.86, except for formic acid, which was 0.62). For the six-year average, the sources of methanol and MEK were similar, with the largest contribution from primary anthropogenic sources  $(47 - 51\%)$  followed by biogenic and background sources. The contributions to acetaldehyde and acetic acid from primary and secondary anthropogenic sources were similar  $(26 - 32\%)$  followed by biogenic sources (approximately  $20\%$ ). In contrast to other OVOCs, acetone was the most affected by regional background concentration  $(37\%)$  which may be because acetone is relatively inactive and widely exists in the atmosphere, and its ground-level background mixing ratios can reach 500 pptv throughout the troposphere under extremely clean conditions (Seinfeld and Pandis, 2016). For formic acid, secondary anthropogenic sources contributed the most  $(34\%)$

Fig. 7 presents the changes in absolute concentrations of OVOCs contributed by different sources during the dry seasons for the six-year study period. To better depict the contribution trends of different OVOC sources, the parameters were normalized (based on

the OVOC concentrations in 2014). From 2014 to 2019, the ambient concentration of OVOCs contributed by primary anthropogenic sources decreased significantly, declining by  $50 - 60\%$ . Among the six OVOC species, the species with the largest decrease in concentration attributed to primary anthropogenic sources was methanol, which decreased by 7 ppb. However, contributions of the other sources to the absolute concentrations of OVOCs did not significantly change over the six-year period. Specifically, annual variations in secondary anthropogenic sources were not significant, indicating that secondary formation of atmospheric OVOCs in this region might be restricted by the atmospheric oxidizing capacity and that precursor emissions are more than abundant.

hydrocarbon and 490 tons of NOx. VOC emission reduction in industrial enterprises and the coating industry occurs mainly through upgrading emission standards and closing down industries with high VOC emissions. For example, furniture manufacturing used to be an important industry in Shenzhen but also a key VOC-emitting industry. From 2010 to 2016, 565 coating production lines were removed or relocated in Shenzhen, reducing VOC emissions by more than 30,000 tons every year. Since 2017, Shenzhen has completely banned solvent paints and adhesives in the manufacturing process and replaced them with water-based paints to further reduce VOC emissions, especially the emissions of aldehydes and ketones commonly used in the production of furniture manufacturing (Ecological Environment Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). As a commonly used tracer of anthropogenic sources of VOCs, the annual average concentration of benzene during the dry season from 2014 to 2019 was 0.98, 0.81, 0.64, 0.61, 0.49, and 0.45 ppb (Figure S5), respectively, reflecting the decreasing trend in primary VOC emissions in recent years. Therefore, the decrease in OVOC concentrations during the dry season over the six-year study period could primarily be attributed to decreases in the contributions from primary anthropogenic sources. However, the contribution of secondary anthropogenic

PTR-MS was used for continuous online monitoring of atmospheric VOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019. The results revealed that OVOCs were the most abundant of 17 species of VOCs measured, comprising  $70 - 78\%$ . The six-year mean concentration for the dry season was  $33.1~\mathrm{ppb}$ , approximately  $13\%$  higher than that for the wet season of  $22.9~\mathrm{ppb}$ . There were diurnal variations in OVOC concentrations, with higher concentrations during the daytime. The diurnal variation in acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O}_3$ , and it had the largest OFP of the OVOC species, indicating that acetaldehyde was an important intermediate of photochemical reactions. The results from source apportionment based on photochemical aging parameterization revealed that contributions of OVOCs from primary anthropogenic sources exhibited an evident declining trend of  $50 - 60\%$  from 2014 to 2019 during the dry season, indicating that the intensive VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas of the sampling site had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. However, to reduce the impact of OVOCs on air quality in Shenzhen, more efforts are needed to control OVOCs from anthropogenic secondary sources by reducing not only their primary VOC precursors but also their complex atmospheric oxidizing capacity, which will finally restrict the production of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA.

content.summary: Figure 4 shows spatial distributions of tropospheric NO2 columns in China during summer 2006, comparing GEOS-Chem model results with OMI satellite observations across six scenarios.
content.insight: The figure highlights the sensitivity of NO2 distribution modeling to different spatial proxies, impacting the accuracy of emission allocations in densely populated regions.

metadata.title: Figure 4. Spatial distributions of summer averaged tropospheric  NO_2  vertical columns modeled by GEOS-Chem based on S1-S6 emissions and scenario-specific data retrieved from the OMI for 2006.
references: The spatial distributions of tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns over China in the summer simulated from six gridded inventories are presented and compared with scenario-specific satellite-observed  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns in Fig. 4. Because all the inventories in model simulations have the same emission totals, differences among S1-S6 reflect differences in the spatial allocations of the total emissions. In general, modeled and observed  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns exhibit similar patterns but different fine structures. All modeled cases can reproduce highly polluted areas over the North China Plain and Yangtze River Delta, whereas many pollution hotspots in these regions are underestimated compared to the satellite data in S1. As discussed above, using the total population distribution as a spatial proxy may have misrepresented the urban-rural gradients of economic development levels and allocated disproportionately large fractions of emissions to rural regions. Consequently, urban emission hotspots may have been underestimated because in China the rural population exceeds the urban population.

Abstract. Spatial proxies used in bottom-up emission inventories to derive the spatial distributions of emissions are usually empirical and involve additional levels of uncertainty. Although uncertainties in current emission inventories have been discussed extensively, uncertainties resulting from improper spatial proxies have rarely been evaluated. In this work, we investigate the impact of spatial proxies on the representation of gridded emissions by comparing six gridded  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission datasets over China developed from the same magnitude of emissions and different spatial proxies. GEOS-Chem-modeled tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns simulated from different gridded emission inventories are compared with satellite-based columns. The results show that differences between modeled and satellite-based  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns are sensitive to the spatial proxies used in the gridded emission inventories. The total population density is less suitable for allocating  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions than nighttime light data because population density tends to allocate more emissions to rural areas. Determining the exact locations of large emission sources could significantly strengthen the correlation between modeled and observed  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns. Using vehicle population and an updated road network for the on-road transport sector could substantially en

In this work, we use  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  as a case to study the influence of spatial proxies on spatial distributions of bottom-up emission inventories because  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  satellite retrieval is less uncertain and the spatial distributions of tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns are similar to those of surface  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions, especially in the summer when the lifetime of  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  is short (Richter et al., 2005; Beirle et al., 2003; Lin, 2012). Based on the same magnitude of  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions, we develop six sets of gridded emission data using different spatial proxies. We then use these gridded emissions and the nested-grid GEOS-Chem model to simulate tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns and compare them with satellite-based observations. The effects of spatial proxies on the modeled  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns and representations of different spatial proxies are evaluated and discussed.

In this study, we use GEOS-Chem version 09-01-02 to simulate tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns over China for 2006. The EDGAR v3 emission inventory (Olivier and Berdowski, 2001) is used for global anthropogenic emissions, and the East-Southeast Asia region is replaced with the INTEX-B inventory (Zhang et al., 2009). We further override the anthropogenic  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission inventory for China using the six datasets described in Sect. 2.2. To remove the effects of the initial concentration fields, a 1-year spin up is conducted. We use averaged summer (June, July, and August (JJA))  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns for this evaluation because the short lifetime of  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  in the summer favors  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  column linkage with local  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions. We average daily  $2\mathrm{h}$  modeled tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns at a local time of 13:00-15:00 and sample the model at grids coincident with the daily satellite pixels used in the final average columns.

The satellite data used in this work come from the Ozone Monitoring Instrument (OMI) aboard the Aura satellite (Levelt et al., 2006).  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  slant column densities are derived using a differential optical absorption spectroscopy (DOAS) algorithm (Platt, 1994; Boersma et al., 2002; Bucsela et al., 2006). The tropospheric slant  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  column densities used in this work are drawn from the Dutch OMI  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  (DOMINO) product (version 2, collection 3) (Boersma et al., 2011) available from the Tropospheric Emission Monitoring Internet Service (TEMIS) (http://www.temis.nl/). The air mass factor (AMF) is a multiplicative factor used to convert slant columns into vertical columns (Palmer et al., 2001). The retrieved tropospheric vertical  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  column is sensitive to the  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical profile used during the AMF calculation. Following Lamsal et al. (2010), we revise the AMF by replacing the original  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  profile with that generated from the nested-grid GEOS-Chem model using S1-S6 emissions described

above. The new  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical profiles have a finer spatial resolution of  $0.667^{\circ}$  long  $\times 0.5^{\circ}$  lat compared to the original resolution of  $3^{\circ}$  long  $\times 2^{\circ}$  lat. Six scenario-specific OMI  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  products are generated for comparison with corresponding model results, and higher resolution prior profiles could reduce the bias by  $3 - 6\%$  between modeled and satellite data for different scenarios. In this work, we restrict the use of OMI pixels to those at a solar zenith angle of  $\leq 70^{\circ}$  and a cloud fraction of  $\leq 0.3$  in the final averaged columns. Pixels at swath edges (five pixels on each side) are rejected to reduce spatial averaging. Finally, each OMI pixel is allocated to  $0.667^{\circ}$  long  $\times 0.5^{\circ}$  lat grids by area weights with corner coordinate information to create daily tropospheric vertical  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  column maps. The retrieved  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns are also resampled from pixels to county averages to draw comparisons with indicators at the county level.

Figure 5 compares modeled and satellite-retrieved tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns by county in China for the analyzed six cases. The first column in Fig. 5 compares the model and satellite data for all districts and counties in China for the summer of 2006. Modeled  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns are generally in good agreement with OMI  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns, with regression slopes varying from 0.78 to 1.01 and  $R^2$  values varying from 0.75 to 0.86. Simulations obtained using S1 substantially underestimate  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns compared to satellite-based columns, especially in densely populated regions. Tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns simulated using S2 present more pollution hotspots compared to S1, particularly in economically developed regions, such as the capital cities in each province. Using nighttime lights as a spatial proxy (S2)

The spatial proxies used when developing gridded emission inventories are empirical and can introduce uncertainties in bottom-up emission inventories. This issue has rarely been evaluated. In this work, we evaluate the effects of spatial proxies on the representations of spatial distributions of emissions using an integrated framework of bottom-up emission inventories, a chemical transport model, and satellite observations. We first develop six sets of gridded  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions for China using the same magnitude of emissions from the MEIC and different spatial proxies. The spatial proxies considered in this study include the following: total population, nighttime lights, locations of power plants, IGDP, vehicle populations, and two different road network datasets. The nested-grid GEOS-Chem model is then used to simulate tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns using the six gridded emissions, and modeled  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns are compared to satellite-based  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns derived from OMI data.

metadata.title: Fig. 7. Observed and simulated monthly average of the daily 1-h maximum  O_3  concentrations ((Base case: anthropogenic emissions + biogenic emissions; NB case: only anthropogenic emissions; NA case: only biogenic emissions,  \mu g m^{-3} ).
metadata.supplement: (a)O_3  simulated by base case in July;(b) Observation;(c) Simulation;(d)  O_3  simulated by NB case in July; (e)  O_3  simulated by NA case in July
references: performance of  $\mathrm{DM1O_3}$  in different regions is evaluated in Table 4. As shown in Table 4 and Fig. 7, the model well reproduced  $\mathrm{DM1O_3}$  in most cities except for slightly underestimates  $\mathrm{DM1O_3}$  concentrations in BTH, YRD, PRD and SCB. The underestimation might be caused by uncertainty of meteorological condition simulated by WRF (such as T2 and DSW discussed in Section 3.1). Besides, the relatively coarse spatial resolution in the model and underestimation of anthropogenic emissions in these megacities may also contribute to the underestimation.

Ozone concentrations mainly peaked in the eastern and southwestern China, particularly in the BTH, YRD, PRD and SCB regions because of their considerably large NOx and VOC emission sources and advantageous meteorological conditions for ozone formation. Even though the effect of biogenic emissions on  $\mathrm{O_3}$  is less profound than that of anthropogenic emissions (as shown in Fig. 7(d)(e)), the BVOC emissions still made a significant contribution to summertime ozone due to the influence of the southerly wind, transporting precursors from high biogenic emission regions in southern China to central China and the BTH area. And the regions where the influence of biogenic emissions is high match with those where ozone formation is VOC limited.

Fig. 9 presents the SOA concentrations simulated by base case, NA and NB case, respectively. In summer, SOA concentrations in central and eastern China typically exceed  $1\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$  and can reach up to  $3\mu \mathrm{g}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ . There is also a relatively high SOA concentration in other southern provinces and the Sichuan Basin. The simulated SOA concentrations are low in northern and northeastern China. Removing anthropogenic VOC emissions in July causes a decrease of SOA in major areas by relative change of approximately  $60\%$  while eliminating biogenic emissions results in an approximately  $70\%$  reduction of the simulated SOA concentrations. Therefore, the removal of biogenic emissions affects both biogenic and anthropogenic compounds of SOA.

metadata.title: Fig. 2. Concentrations of VOC components during dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.
references: A total of 17 species of VOCs were monitored in this study by PTR-MS from 2014 to 2019, including 6 species classified as OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, formic acid, acetic acid, and MEK); 5 aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene, styrene, C8 and C9 aromatics); 3 classified as biogenic species (isoprene, MVK + MACR, and monoterpenes); and 2 species that, in addition to benzene, were measured as tracers (acetonitrile and DMS). The compounds, the respective  $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{z}$  of their parent ion signal, and their detection limits are provided in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 2, the average of the sum of the VOC concentrations in the dry season over the six years ranged from 25.3 to 41.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 33.1 ppbv; for the wet season, the average value ranged from 23.0 to 39.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 29.4 ppbv. In terms of the proportion of each component, in the six years of dry and wet seasons, OVOCs (70–78%) accounted for the largest proportion, followed by aromatic hydrocarbons (16–26%), whereas the concentrations of biogenic VOCs (BVOCs) and tracers were relatively low (less than 10%). Figure S6 shows the time series of 17 species of VOCs during the sampling period. To evaluate the concentrations of OVOCs in this study, comparative results of the concentrations of six OVOCs in five urban areas in China are listed in Table 1. Compared to Guangzhou and Hong Kong, two other mega-cities in the PRD region, the concentrations of acetaldehyde, acetone, and MEK in Shenzhen were higher than those in Hong

Oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) are important precursors and intermediate products of atmospheric photochemical reactions, which can promote the formation of secondary pollutants such as ozone  $(\mathrm{O}_3)$  and secondary organic aerosol (SOA). However, there have been few studies on the sources of and long-term variation in ambient OVOCs. This study combined sensitive, near real-time measurements of VOCs by proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) with an improved photochemical age parameterization method to quantify daytime sources of OVOCs in an urban atmosphere in China from 2014 to 2019, permitting the observation of the impacts of emission control strategies that were implemented during this period. Temporal variation in six key OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), formic acid, and acetic acid) were observed. The sum of concentrations of OVOCs was averagely  $13\%$  higher during the dry season (November to April), when winds transported polluted air masses to Shenzhen from the continent, than during the wet season, and peak diurnal levels occurred during the daytime year-round due to photochemical production and higher daytime anthropogenic emissions. The average dry season concentration of OVOCs declined from a peak of 30.3 ppb in 2015 to 18.7 ppb in 2019. The results of source apportionment showed that primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to methanol, MEK, and acetic acid  $(32 - 51\%)$ ; the dominant sources of acetaldehyde and formic acid were both primary and secondary anthropogenic sources; and biomass burning contributed a small fraction  $(5 - 11\%)$  to the six OVOCs. From 2014 to 2019, contributions from primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs decreased significantly by  $50 - 60\%$  due to intensive pollution control measures in Shenzhen, whereas pollution control measures had no observable impact on secondary OVOCs, indicating their formation was not limited by availability of their primary VOC precursors.

As presented in Fig. 1, Shenzhen is located in the PRD of Guangdong Province, China. The sampling site for this study was located on the campus of the Shenzhen Graduate School of Peking University (PKUSZ,  $22.60^{\circ}\mathrm{N}$ ,  $113.97^{\circ}\mathrm{E}$ ) in western Shenzhen, which is predominantly surrounded by green areas, teaching buildings and apartments. No evident anthropogenic emission sources of air pollutants surrounded the sampling site, except a highway approximately  $100\mathrm{m}$  away. Previous studies have shown that the concentrations of air pollutants such as  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and VOCs at this site were approximately equal to their average concentrations in the entire PRD region (Louie et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2018). Therefore, this site was considered suitable for long-term observation of trends in air pollutants in the PRD region. Table S1 summarizes the effective days of VOCs observed in each month from 2014 to 2019, with 1169 days in total over the six years of sampling. During the six-year sampling period, the loss of data was mainly due to instrument failure and bad weather, especially in the wet season due to frequent rainfall, resulting in a higher rate of missing data during

Shenzhen belongs to the subtropical marine monsoon climate zone. Marked differences in the four seasons were not evident, and the observation time each year was slightly different; therefore, this study divided the entire year into a dry season and a wet season according to the monthly temperature and precipitation. As presented in Figure S1, the period from May to October, with higher temperatures and abundant precipitation, was defined as the wet season, and the period from November to April, with less precipitation, was defined as the dry season. The meteorological data are from the Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality (Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). In addition to the differences in temperature and precipitation, the sources of air masses in Shenzhen during the wet and dry seasons were also very different. Taking January and July 2018 as an example, the 48-h backward trajectories at  $100\mathrm{m}$  above ground level in Shenzhen  $(113.9^{\circ}\mathrm{E}, 22.6^{\circ}\mathrm{N})$  were calculated beginning at 12:00 UTC using the Hybrid Single Particle Lagrangian Integrated Trajectory (HYSPLIT) model (Draxler and Rolph, 2003). The results show that the origin of the air mass passing through Shenzhen in different seasons was very different: during the dry season, the air mass mainly came from the northern mainland, and during the wet season, it mainly came from the South China Sea, which has also been explained in detail in our previous studies (Zou et al., 2017). In addition, referencing existing studies of air pollution in the Pearl River Delta, many scholars have adopted a method of dividing the wet and dry seasons (Cui et al., 2015). Figure S2 shows the changes in major meteorological parameters during the six-year observation period in Shenzhen. Analysis of major meteorological parameters in Shenzhen (Fig S2) did not indicate significant interannual variability in temperature, rainfall, humidity or wind conditions that could have substantially influenced the long-term variation in atmospheric pollutants observed in this study.

It should be emphasized that because the sampling data of this study span six years, in addition to the above assumptions, one more assumption should be added: (4) during the sampling period, there was no significant change in the ratio of OVOC anthropogenic emissions to tracers (benzene) in Shenzhen. The emission reduction strategy of Shenzhen is to reduce emission sources of pollutants as a whole, such as replacing gasoline vehicles with electric vehicles and closing down or moving VOC-emitting enterprises to other areas. These measures have reduced emissions of VOCs, but the emission ratio among specific VOC species is relatively stable. Toluene/benzene (T/B) was considered a good indicator to judge the contribution of motor vehicle sources and solvent use to VOC emissions (Nelson and Quigley, 1984; Wang et al., 2002; Araizaga et al., 2013). Figure S5 shows the changes in the concentrations of benzene and toluene and T/B from 2014 to 2019. The concentrations of benzene and toluene in the six years showed a downward trend, while T/B remained at approximately 5, which also indicated

In this study, the six-year average of VOCs in the dry season was approximately  $13\%$  higher than that in the wet season. As shown in Figure S7, the concentrations of OVCs, aromatic hydrocarbons and tracers in the wet season were lower than those in the dry season, while the BVOC concentration was higher in the wet season. As a highly developed urban area, Shenzhen was active in anthropogenic activities year-round, and there was no obvious seasonal difference in anthropogenic emissions. Therefore, it is more likely that seasonal meteorological differences lead to variations in VOC concentrations between wet and dry seasons. For example, the air masses passing through Shenzhen during the dry season were predominantly from northern inland regions, and the concentration of pollutants carried by inland air masses was higher than that of marine air masses (He et al., 2011; Huang et al., 2020). In addition, the dry season had a lower boundary layer and less precipitation, which was also conducive to the accumulation of pollutants. However, the concentration of BVOCs during the wet season was significantly higher than during the dry season, as shown in Figure S7 (g), (h) and (i), primarily because the higher light levels and solar radiation during the wet season led to accelerated release rates of VOCs by plants (Loreto et al., 2010). It should be pointed out that the data collection frequency in the dry season  $(\sim 73\%)$  was much higher than that in the wet season  $(\sim 35\%)$ , which may lead to relatively higher uncertainty of the conclusion for the wet season.

The average diurnal variations in mixing ratios of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and selected VOCs during the dry and wet seasons are shown in Fig. 4. To more clearly compare the diurnal variation trends of the pollutants, all concentrations were normalized (divided by their respective average concentrations). The diurnal variations of the same species during the dry and wet seasons were similar, but  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and OVOCs rose relatively more during the daytime during the warmer wet season. The diurnal variation of benzene showed two peaks corresponding to the morning and afternoon traffic emissions, but the concentration decreased in the afternoon, which may

be due to the dilution caused by the uplift of the planetary boundary layer (PBL). However, the six OVOCs exhibited a significant upward trend during the daytime. These results suggested that photochemical production and/or daytime anthropogenic activities related to OVC emissions were notable and could overwhelm photochemical loss. The diurnal variation trend of acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O_3}$ , exhibiting a greater difference in concentration between day and night during the wet season, indicating that the majority of acetaldehyde originated from daytime secondary generation (Chang et al., 2019). The daytime splits of the diurnal curves of methanol, MEK, and acetic acid, similar to benzene, implied that they could have relatively larger contributions from primary emissions sources, such as motor vehicle emissions. (Guo et al., 2011, Zheng et al., 2013). The diurnal variations in VOCs in our previous two individual campaigns, which were carried out during the dry season (Huang et al., 2019, 2020), were almost the same as those in the dry season in this study.
Fig. 4. Normalized average diurnal variations in  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and typical VOCs during the dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019 (shaded part:  $\pm \mathrm{SD}$ ).
Fig. 5. The average mixing ratios of the measured VOCs and calculated OFPs in the dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.

The parameterization method based on photochemical aging described in section 2.4 was used to analyze the sources of OVOCs during the daytime (8:00-20:00). Referencing the source analysis results for the OVOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019 (in Table 2), the measured concentrations of six OVOC species monitored by PTR-MS were in good agreement with the simulated concentrations, and the measured values of each OVOC species were highly correlated with the simulated values (R was between 0.71 and 0.86, except for formic acid, which was 0.62). For the six-year average, the sources of methanol and MEK were similar, with the largest contribution from primary anthropogenic sources  $(47 - 51\%)$  followed by biogenic and background sources. The contributions to acetaldehyde and acetic acid from primary and secondary anthropogenic sources were similar  $(26 - 32\%)$  followed by biogenic sources (approximately  $20\%$ ). In contrast to other OVOCs, acetone was the most affected by regional background concentration  $(37\%)$  which may be because acetone is relatively inactive and widely exists in the atmosphere, and its ground-level background mixing ratios can reach 500 pptv throughout the troposphere under extremely clean conditions (Seinfeld and Pandis, 2016). For formic acid, secondary anthropogenic sources contributed the most  $(34\%)$

Fig. 6 shows the trends in the relative contributions to OVOCs from 2014 to 2019 during the dry and wet seasons. In general, during the six years from 2014 to 2019, primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to OVOCs during Shenzhen's dry season, but the proportion exhibited a decreasing trend year over year, and the proportion during the six-year period decreased by approximately  $15\%$  overall. This continuous decreasing trend indicates that the VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas, where most of the industry and traffic of Shenzhen is located, had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. The OVOC source compositions during the wet season, when clean marine air masses from the south dominated the sampling site, did not exhibit a clear year-by-year trend. The contributions of secondary anthropogenic sources to OVOC concentrations (except that of methanol) during the wet season were significantly higher (approximately 1.3-1.6 times greater) than those during the dry season, indicating that secondary transformation of NMHCs during the wet season contributed greatly to OVOC concentrations. Biogenic sources reasonably contributed a larger proportion of OVOC concentrations during the wet season, whereas biomass burning contributed a larger fraction of acetaldehyde during the dry season.

hydrocarbon and 490 tons of NOx. VOC emission reduction in industrial enterprises and the coating industry occurs mainly through upgrading emission standards and closing down industries with high VOC emissions. For example, furniture manufacturing used to be an important industry in Shenzhen but also a key VOC-emitting industry. From 2010 to 2016, 565 coating production lines were removed or relocated in Shenzhen, reducing VOC emissions by more than 30,000 tons every year. Since 2017, Shenzhen has completely banned solvent paints and adhesives in the manufacturing process and replaced them with water-based paints to further reduce VOC emissions, especially the emissions of aldehydes and ketones commonly used in the production of furniture manufacturing (Ecological Environment Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). As a commonly used tracer of anthropogenic sources of VOCs, the annual average concentration of benzene during the dry season from 2014 to 2019 was 0.98, 0.81, 0.64, 0.61, 0.49, and 0.45 ppb (Figure S5), respectively, reflecting the decreasing trend in primary VOC emissions in recent years. Therefore, the decrease in OVOC concentrations during the dry season over the six-year study period could primarily be attributed to decreases in the contributions from primary anthropogenic sources. However, the contribution of secondary anthropogenic
Fig. 7. Change in ambient concentrations of OVOCs contributed by different sources during the dry seasons of 2014-2019.

PTR-MS was used for continuous online monitoring of atmospheric VOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019. The results revealed that OVOCs were the most abundant of 17 species of VOCs measured, comprising  $70 - 78\%$ . The six-year mean concentration for the dry season was  $33.1~\mathrm{ppb}$ , approximately  $13\%$  higher than that for the wet season of  $22.9~\mathrm{ppb}$ . There were diurnal variations in OVOC concentrations, with higher concentrations during the daytime. The diurnal variation in acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O}_3$ , and it had the largest OFP of the OVOC species, indicating that acetaldehyde was an important intermediate of photochemical reactions. The results from source apportionment based on photochemical aging parameterization revealed that contributions of OVOCs from primary anthropogenic sources exhibited an evident declining trend of  $50 - 60\%$  from 2014 to 2019 during the dry season, indicating that the intensive VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas of the sampling site had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. However, to reduce the impact of OVOCs on air quality in Shenzhen, more efforts are needed to control OVOCs from anthropogenic secondary sources by reducing not only their primary VOC precursors but also their complex atmospheric oxidizing capacity, which will finally restrict the production of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA.

metadata.title: Fig. 6. Percentages  \%  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), OH loss rate  (L_{OH)} , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP).
references: Fig. 6 shows the contributions of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio. The combustion sources contributed most (39.0% or  $23.9 \pm 13.6$  ppbv) to the total VOCs in winter, followed by vehicle emissions (22.5%,  $14.0 \pm 11.3$  ppbv), gasoline evaporation (14.2%,  $8.83 \pm 7.11$  ppbv), industrial sources (12.6%,  $7.92 \pm 10.2$  ppbv), and solvent usage (11.6%,  $6.98 \pm 5.65$  ppbv), respectively.

As shown in Fig. 6, industrial sources contributed  $32.2\%$  to the total OH loss rate, followed by solvent usage  $(21.4\%)$ , combustion sources  $(20.4\%)$ , vehicle emissions  $(19.4\%)$ , and gasoline evaporation  $(6.61\%)$  respectively. Usually, vehicle emissions or petrochemical industrial emissions dominate man-made VOC reactivity in urban areas. For instance, motor vehicle emissions (dominated by high fractions of aromatics and alkenes) contributed to  $50\%$  of the calculated VOC reactivity in Pasadena (Borbon et al., 2013). Similarly,  $\mathrm{C}_2 \sim \mathrm{C}_6$  alkenes and aromatics from the petrochemical refinery and motor vehicles accounted for  $42\%$  of the total calculated VOC reactivity  $(7.5~\mathrm{s}^{-1})$  in the Houston/ Galveston Bay region (Gilman et al., 2009). The percentages of vehicle emissions and industrial sources to OH loss rate in this study were lower than those in the two above researches. However, in this study, the VOC reactivity  $(2.79~\mathrm{s}^{-1})$  contributed by vehicle emissions was higher than the traffic-related reactivity  $(1.2~\mathrm{s}^{-1})$  in the Northern Front Range Metropolitan Area of Colorado (Abeleira et al., 2017).

For the OFP of different sources (Fig. 6), vehicle emissions had the largest contribution  $(27.8\%)$ , followed by industrial sources  $(25.4\%)$ , solvent usage  $(20.9\%)$ , combustion sources  $(18.9\%)$ , and gasoline evaporation  $(7.04\%)$ , respectively. The OFP of vehicle emissions in this study was comparable to a previous study in Wuhan that vehicle exhausts accounted for  $23.4 \pm 1.3\%$  of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  formation (Lyu et al., 2016). However, the contribution of industrial sources to OFP was lower than

Volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are important contributors to atmospheric chemical reactivity rate  $(\mathrm{L_{OH}})$ , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol formation potential (SOAP). The purpose of this study was to determine the contributions of different VOC sources to  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP using positive matrix factorization (PMF) model. Hourly observation of ambient VOCs at a megacity of Central China was conducted for one month in winter. The  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP of VOC species were firstly estimated. The PMF model was then used to apportion their sources. Five VOC sources including solvent usage, gasoline evaporation, combustion sources, vehicle emissions, and industrial sources were apportioned with their average contributions of  $11.6\%$ ,  $14.2\%$ ,  $39.0\%$ ,  $22.5\%$ , and  $12.6\%$ , respectively. Industrial sources were the highest contributors to the  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$  and SOAP with average contributions of  $32.2\%$  and  $32.8\%$ , respectively, while vehicle emissions contributed most to the OFP  $(27.8\%)$ . This study highlighted that the VOCs and ozone pollution control should give priority to the reactivity (OFP and SOAP) based strategies rather than concentration contribution-based strategies.

Alkanes  
Fig. 3. Individual contributions  $(\%)$  of different VOC species (outer circular bar plots) and mean contributions  $(\%)$  (inner donut charts) of alkanes, alkenes, acetylene, and aromatics to the OH loss rate (a), ozone formation potential (b), and secondary organic aerosol potential (c).

Fig. 7. Percentages  $(\%)$  of different VOC sources to the total VOC mixing ratio (MR), reactivity  $\mathrm{(L_{OH})}$ , ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP) under different  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentration ranges.

To better understand the differences in levels and reactivity of VOC sources under different air quality, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels were classified into several groups and the source contributions, OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP of VOCs were summarized in Fig. 7. Generally, with the increase of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels from clean to heavy pollution, the VOC levels also increased from  $40.8 \pm 17.6$  ppbv to  $89.6 \pm 26.3$  ppbv. The same phenomenon was also reported elsewhere (Han et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2017; Hui et al., 2019). Fig. 7 clearly shows that the high  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution was characterized by an increase of industrial sources, which accounted for up to  $27.3\%$ ,  $56.2\%$ ,  $48.4\%$ , and  $57.0\%$  of VOCs mixing ratio, OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP, respectively. The results here were similar to a previous study that the percentage of industrial sources increased significantly with the increase in VOC levels and this source dominated the VOC origins during an entire severe pollution episode (Hui et al.,

2019). On the contrary, the contributions of combustion sources, vehicle emissions, and solvent usage to OH loss rate, OFP, and SOAP decreased with the increase of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  levels. Despite a decreasing trend of combustion sources to the VOC mixing ratio from clean to pollution, it still dominated the VOC origins during the heavy pollution period. Therefore, to alleviate the VOCs pollution, it should prior control combustion sources. From the perspective of VOC reactivity, OFP, and SOAP, reducing VOC emissions from industrial sources would have benefits to both the reduction of VOC levels and SOA during pollution episodes. The results here contributed to better-developing countermeasures of alleviating VOCs, ozone, and particulate matter pollutions.

The limitations of this study were from several aspects: one is the limited known chemistry properties of VOC species; the other is the underestimation of the current modelling; and the third is the assumption of the PMF model. For instance, the ozone reactivity scales for 118 VOC species under different  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  conditions were summarized by Carter (2013). Similarly, Atkinson and Arey (2003) summarized the atmospheric degradation of about 190 VOC species (known as the rate constant  $k_{OH}$ ). A list of the SOAPs relative to toluene for 113 VOC species was also reported by Derwent et al. (2010). Despite the great efforts have been made to study the photochemical characteristics of the VOC species, some VOC species' properties are still unknown. They may be important to the atmospheric reactivity, ozone, and secondary aerosol formation potentials. Limited species (i.e., 33 species in this study) introduced into the PMF model suggested the underestimation of  $\mathrm{L_{OH}}$ , OFP, and SOAP. For SOA modelling, the SOA yield method using chamber study or simulation using volatility basis sets (VBS) underestimated the SOA mass (Chen et al., 2017). For the estimation of OFP, despite the MIR method considers the chemical mechanisms and impacts of the  $\mathrm{VOC} / \mathrm{NO}_x$  ratio on ozone production, it may also introduce simulation uncertainties (Ran et al., 2009).

the flaws and uncertainty existed, this study also provided an additional approach to understand the contributions of various VOC sources to the reactivity of OH loss rate, ozone formation potential (OFP), and secondary organic aerosol potential (SOAP) using the PMF model.

metadata.title: Fig. 4. Normalized average diurnal variations in  O_3  and typical VOCs during the dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019 (shaded part:  \pm SD ).
references: The average diurnal variations in mixing ratios of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and selected VOCs during the dry and wet seasons are shown in Fig. 4. To more clearly compare the diurnal variation trends of the pollutants, all concentrations were normalized (divided by their respective average concentrations). The diurnal variations of the same species during the dry and wet seasons were similar, but  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and OVOCs rose relatively more during the daytime during the warmer wet season. The diurnal variation of benzene showed two peaks corresponding to the morning and afternoon traffic emissions, but the concentration decreased in the afternoon, which may

Oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) are important precursors and intermediate products of atmospheric photochemical reactions, which can promote the formation of secondary pollutants such as ozone  $(\mathrm{O}_3)$  and secondary organic aerosol (SOA). However, there have been few studies on the sources of and long-term variation in ambient OVOCs. This study combined sensitive, near real-time measurements of VOCs by proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) with an improved photochemical age parameterization method to quantify daytime sources of OVOCs in an urban atmosphere in China from 2014 to 2019, permitting the observation of the impacts of emission control strategies that were implemented during this period. Temporal variation in six key OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), formic acid, and acetic acid) were observed. The sum of concentrations of OVOCs was averagely  $13\%$  higher during the dry season (November to April), when winds transported polluted air masses to Shenzhen from the continent, than during the wet season, and peak diurnal levels occurred during the daytime year-round due to photochemical production and higher daytime anthropogenic emissions. The average dry season concentration of OVOCs declined from a peak of 30.3 ppb in 2015 to 18.7 ppb in 2019. The results of source apportionment showed that primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to methanol, MEK, and acetic acid  $(32 - 51\%)$ ; the dominant sources of acetaldehyde and formic acid were both primary and secondary anthropogenic sources; and biomass burning contributed a small fraction  $(5 - 11\%)$  to the six OVOCs. From 2014 to 2019, contributions from primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs decreased significantly by  $50 - 60\%$  due to intensive pollution control measures in Shenzhen, whereas pollution control measures had no observable impact on secondary OVOCs, indicating their formation was not limited by availability of their primary VOC precursors.

A total of 17 species of VOCs were monitored in this study by PTR-MS from 2014 to 2019, including 6 species classified as OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, formic acid, acetic acid, and MEK); 5 aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene, styrene, C8 and C9 aromatics); 3 classified as biogenic species (isoprene, MVK + MACR, and monoterpenes); and 2 species that, in addition to benzene, were measured as tracers (acetonitrile and DMS). The compounds, the respective  $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{z}$  of their parent ion signal, and their detection limits are provided in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 2, the average of the sum of the VOC concentrations in the dry season over the six years ranged from 25.3 to 41.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 33.1 ppbv; for the wet season, the average value ranged from 23.0 to 39.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 29.4 ppbv. In terms of the proportion of each component, in the six years of dry and wet seasons, OVOCs (70–78%) accounted for the largest proportion, followed by aromatic hydrocarbons (16–26%), whereas the concentrations of biogenic VOCs (BVOCs) and tracers were relatively low (less than 10%). Figure S6 shows the time series of 17 species of VOCs during the sampling period. To evaluate the concentrations of OVOCs in this study, comparative results of the concentrations of six OVOCs in five urban areas in China are listed in Table 1. Compared to Guangzhou and Hong Kong, two other mega-cities in the PRD region, the concentrations of acetaldehyde, acetone, and MEK in Shenzhen were higher than those in Hong

In this study, the six-year average of VOCs in the dry season was approximately  $13\%$  higher than that in the wet season. As shown in Figure S7, the concentrations of OVCs, aromatic hydrocarbons and tracers in the wet season were lower than those in the dry season, while the BVOC concentration was higher in the wet season. As a highly developed urban area, Shenzhen was active in anthropogenic activities year-round, and there was no obvious seasonal difference in anthropogenic emissions. Therefore, it is more likely that seasonal meteorological differences lead to variations in VOC concentrations between wet and dry seasons. For example, the air masses passing through Shenzhen during the dry season were predominantly from northern inland regions, and the concentration of pollutants carried by inland air masses was higher than that of marine air masses (He et al., 2011; Huang et al., 2020). In addition, the dry season had a lower boundary layer and less precipitation, which was also conducive to the accumulation of pollutants. However, the concentration of BVOCs during the wet season was significantly higher than during the dry season, as shown in Figure S7 (g), (h) and (i), primarily because the higher light levels and solar radiation during the wet season led to accelerated release rates of VOCs by plants (Loreto et al., 2010). It should be pointed out that the data collection frequency in the dry season  $(\sim 73\%)$  was much higher than that in the wet season  $(\sim 35\%)$ , which may lead to relatively higher uncertainty of the conclusion for the wet season.

be due to the dilution caused by the uplift of the planetary boundary layer (PBL). However, the six OVOCs exhibited a significant upward trend during the daytime. These results suggested that photochemical production and/or daytime anthropogenic activities related to OVC emissions were notable and could overwhelm photochemical loss. The diurnal variation trend of acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O_3}$ , exhibiting a greater difference in concentration between day and night during the wet season, indicating that the majority of acetaldehyde originated from daytime secondary generation (Chang et al., 2019). The daytime splits of the diurnal curves of methanol, MEK, and acetic acid, similar to benzene, implied that they could have relatively larger contributions from primary emissions sources, such as motor vehicle emissions. (Guo et al., 2011, Zheng et al., 2013). The diurnal variations in VOCs in our previous two individual campaigns, which were carried out during the dry season (Huang et al., 2019, 2020), were almost the same as those in the dry season in this study.
Fig. 5. The average mixing ratios of the measured VOCs and calculated OFPs in the dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.

PTR-MS was used for continuous online monitoring of atmospheric VOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019. The results revealed that OVOCs were the most abundant of 17 species of VOCs measured, comprising  $70 - 78\%$ . The six-year mean concentration for the dry season was  $33.1~\mathrm{ppb}$ , approximately  $13\%$  higher than that for the wet season of  $22.9~\mathrm{ppb}$ . There were diurnal variations in OVOC concentrations, with higher concentrations during the daytime. The diurnal variation in acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O}_3$ , and it had the largest OFP of the OVOC species, indicating that acetaldehyde was an important intermediate of photochemical reactions. The results from source apportionment based on photochemical aging parameterization revealed that contributions of OVOCs from primary anthropogenic sources exhibited an evident declining trend of  $50 - 60\%$  from 2014 to 2019 during the dry season, indicating that the intensive VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas of the sampling site had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. However, to reduce the impact of OVOCs on air quality in Shenzhen, more efforts are needed to control OVOCs from anthropogenic secondary sources by reducing not only their primary VOC precursors but also their complex atmospheric oxidizing capacity, which will finally restrict the production of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA.

metadata.title: Figure 12. Spatial distributions of the optimized scale factors for annual Chinese NMVOC emissions, relative to the a priori emission estimates, for the four inversion experiments.
references: Figure 12 shows the spatial distribution of the scale factors for the Chinese annual NMVOC emissions from each of the four inversion experiments relative to the a priori emission estimates. The use of GOME-2A formaldehyde and glyoxal observations as constraints in IE-1 led to a domain-wide increase in biogenic NMVOC emissions, except in the northeast. IE-1 also found an increase in biomass burning emissions over the NCP in June. In contrast, anthropogenic NMVOC emissions were slightly reduced over northeast, north, and southwest China. In IE-3, the annual NMVOC emissions over eastern China increased for all three sources, due to constraints exerted by the scaled-up GOME-2A formaldehyde VCDs. The optimized emission scale fac

Abstract. We used the GEOS-Chem model and its adjoint to quantify Chinese non-methane volatile organic compound (NMVOC) emissions for the year 2007, using the tropospheric column concentrations of formaldehyde and glyoxal observed by the Global Ozone Monitoring Experiment 2A (GOME-2A) instrument and the Ozone Monitoring Instrument (OMI) as quantitative constraints. We conducted a series of inversion experiments using different combinations of satellite observations to explore their impacts on the top-down emission estimates. Our top-down estimates for Chinese annual total NMVOC emissions were 30.7 to 49.5 (average 41.9)  $\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$ , including 16.4 to 23.6 (average 20.2)  $\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  from anthropogenic sources, 12.2 to 22.8 (average 19.2)  $\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  from biogenic sources, and 2.08 to 3.13 (average 2.48)  $\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  from biomass burning. In comparison, the a priori estimate for Chinese annual total NMVOC emissions was  $38.3\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$ , including  $18.8\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  from anthropogenic sources,  $17.3\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  from biogenic sources, and  $2.27\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  from biomass burning. The simultaneous use of glyoxal and formaldehyde observations helped distinguish the NMVOC species from different sources and was essential in constraining anthropogenic emissions. Our four in

version experiments consistently showed that the Chinese anthropogenic emissions of NMVOC precursors of glyoxal were larger than the a priori estimates. Our top-down estimates for Chinese annual emission of anthropogenic aromatics (benzene, toluene, and xylene) ranged from 5.5 to  $7.9\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$ ,  $2\%$  to  $46\%$  larger than the estimate of the a priori emission inventory  $(5.4\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1})$ . Three out of our four inversion experiments indicated that the seasonal variation in Chinese NMVOC emissions was significantly stronger than indicated in the a priori inventory. Model simulations driven by the average of our top-down NMVOC emission estimates (which had a stronger seasonal variation than the a priori) showed that surface afternoon ozone concentrations over eastern China increased by  $1 - 8\mathrm{ppb}$  in June and decreased by  $1 - 10\mathrm{ppb}$  in December relative to the simulations using the a priori emissions and were in better agreement with measurements. We concluded that the satellite observations of formaldehyde and glyoxal together provided quantitative constraints on the emissions and source types of NMVOCs over China and improved our understanding on regional chemistry.

Emissions of trace species are traditionally estimated in a "bottom-up" manner using activity data and emission factors, but these bottom-up estimates are sometimes susceptible to large uncertainties. This is especially true for NMVOC emissions in developing countries such as China because (1) a wide range of species, source activities, and technologies are involved (Q. Zhang et al., 2009; Kurokawa et al., 2013; Li et al., 2014; Qiu et al., 2014); (2) locally representative emission factors are often not measured (Wei et al., 2008; Zhao et al., 2011); and (3) reliable activity data are often incomplete, particularly for small-scale industries, residential activities, and agricultural waste burning (Q. Zhang et al., 2009). Bottom-up estimates for total annual Chinese NMVOC emissions for the years 2005 to 2012 ranged from 31 to  $57\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  (Guenther et al., 2006; Bo et al., 2008; Q. Zhang et al., 2009; van der Werf et al., 2010, 2017; Cao et al., 2011; Huang et al., 2012; Kurokawa et al., 2013; Li et al., 2014; Stavrakou et al., 2014; Sindelarova et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2017; Granier et al., 2017). Such large uncertainties in the emission estimates of Chinese NMVOCs have led to great difficulty in evaluating their impacts on regional chemistry (Han et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2014).

transportation, power generation, and residential activities, was compiled using monthly Chinese provincial activity data and a combination of Chinese and western emission factors. The estimated Chinese annual anthropogenic emission of NMVOCs was  $18.8\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$ , including  $63\%$  from industries,  $26\%$  from residential activities,  $10\%$  from transportation, and  $1\%$  from power generation. The estimated annual Chinese anthropogenic emission of aromatics was  $5.4\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$ , including  $73\%$  from industries,  $15\%$  from residential activities,  $9\%$  from transportation, and  $3\%$  from power generation. Previous estimates of Chinese anthropogenic NMVOC emissions for the years 2005 to 2012 ranged from 12.7 to  $35.5\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$ , with aromatics emissions ranging from 2.4 to  $13.4\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  (Bo et al., 2008; Q. Zhang et al., 2009; Cao et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2012; Kurokawa et al., 2013; Li et al., 2014, 2017; Stavrakou et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2017; Granier et al., 2017). We therefore estimated the uncertainty for the a priori Chinese anthropogenic NMVOC emission estimates to be a factor of 2. As such, we did not scale the MEIC Chinese NMVOC emissions to the year 2007 because the uncertainty in the emission estimates was much larger than the differences in emissions between the years 2007 and 2010 (Chinese anthropogenic NMVOC emissions increased  $14\%$  from 2006 to 2010 according to Li et al., 2017). The spatial distribution of Chinese
