metadata.title: Fig. 5. Contributions of VOC groups emitted from SFN, SFM, OLM, and BLM to OFP.
references: The contribution of alkanes, alkenes, alkynes and aromatics to OFP from the different segments within coking plants are shown in Fig. 5. Alkenes were the largest contributor to OFP in SFN and aromatics were the largest contributor to OFP in OLM, accounting for  $94.51\%$  and  $70.18\%$  of the total OFP, respectively. These findings are consistent with the concentration characteristics of VOCs. Then, alkenes and aromatics in SFM and BLM exhibited the most abundant contribution to OFP, accounting for  $49.94\%$  and  $45.92\%$ , and  $43.33\%$  and  $40.29\%$  of the total OFP, respectively. A difference was observed between the characteristic contribution to OFP and the concentration characteristics, suggesting that both the concentration and reactivity of VOCs should be considered in order to reduce the ozone formation of VOCs emitted from coking processes.

The overall source profiles were established using the average weight fraction for different coking plant emission segments (SFN, SFM, OLM, and BLM) to illustrate the emission characteristics of VOCs in different types of coking plant. A total of 47 VOC species developed by the U.S. EPA PAMS program, which exhibit high reactivity in photochemical reactions, were quantified in this study. The average mass concentration of total VOCs from SFN, SFM, OLM, and

All monitored VOCs species were categorized into four groups, i.e., alkanes, alkenes, alkynes and aromatics (Table S2). As shown in Fig. 2, aromatics were the most abundant compounds of SFM, OLM, and BLM, accounting for  $69.17\%$ ,  $72.57\%$ , and  $45.97\%$  of the total VOCs, respectively. The VOC patterns which is mainly characterized by aromatics from different emission segments are similar in the same coking plant and that fugitive leakage should not be ignored during the manufacturing process. On the contrary, the proportion of alkenes to total VOCs was  $70.13\%$  for SFN, implying that the overall source profile varies significantly in different plants with different production engineering.
Fig. 3. VOC profiles of (a) stack flue gas (SFN and SFM) and (b) fugitive emissions (OLM and BLM). The ID number of each VOCs species is listed in Table S2.
Fig. 4. EFs of major VOCs species and the four major groups emitted from SFN and SFM (22DMC4A: 2,2-dimethylbutane).

The OFP was used to assess the chemical reactivity of VOCs from different emission segments, which revealed the priority control emission source according to ozone formation. In this study, the OFP values of SFN, SFM, OLM, and BLM were  $80.26\mathrm{mg}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ,  $1.91\mathrm{mg}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ,  $7.22\mathrm{mg}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , and  $0.21\mathrm{mg}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , respectively. SFN with the higher emission intensity of VOCs ( $11.65\mathrm{mg}\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ) and consequently to higher ozone formation on the atmosphere should be regarded as a priority emission source. However, the OFP of OLM was considerably greater than that of other sources in the mechanical coking plant, suggesting that control of leakage from the coke oven would substantially reduce the formation of ozone.

from the oven (OLM) and from byproducts (BLM) at the mechanical plant. The primary components in the SFN were ethylene, 1-butene, benzene, acetylene, and 2,2-dimethylbutane, whereas those in the SFM were styrene, benzene, and ethylene. The OLM was dominated by benzene, toluene, ethane,  $m$ -xylene, and ethylene, and the BLM, by benzene, propane, ethane, ethylene,  $n$ -pentane,  $n$ -butane, isobutene, 1-butene, toluene, and propylene. These results imply that acetylene and styrene can serve as indicators of non-recovery and mechanical coking processes, respectively. Furthermore, low-molecular-weight hydrocarbons (C2-C4) are synergetic indicators of byproduct emissions at coking plants. The VOCs emission factors for the SFN and SFM were  $96 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke and  $0.4 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke, respectively, demonstrating that VOCs in the stack flue gas of non-recovery coking plants should be controlled due to their high emission intensity. By determining the VOC source profile and emission factor for the SFN, this study provides crucial reference data for the existing source profile library and enables us to assess domestic VOC emissions generated by the coking process. The highest source reactivity was exhibited by the SFN ( $80.26 \mathrm{mg} \mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), followed by the OLM ( $7.22 \mathrm{mg} \mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), which suggests that controlling VOCs in the stack flue gas at non-recovery plants and in the fugitive oven emissions at mechanical plants will greatly reduce the conversion of these pollutants into ozone. Finally, replacing non-recovery coking plants with mechanical ones in China will decrease VOC emissions and significantly improve the ambient air quality.

metadata.title: Figure 5. Monthly mean glyoxal VCDs over China from January to June. For each month, the panels from left to right show (a) glyoxal VCDs observed by GOME-2A, (b) glyoxal VCDs simulated by the model using a priori emission estimates, (c) the a posteriori glyoxal VCDs from IE-1, and (d) the a posteriori glyoxal VCDs from IE-3. All model results were sampled at GOME-2A overpass time.
references: Figure 6. Same as Fig. 5 but for July to December. Also shown are ground-based MAX-DOAS measurements at 09:30 LT at Back Garden (July mean, inverted triangles).

The first and second terms on the right-hand side of Eq. (1) represented the penalty error and the prediction error, respectively.  $x$ , which we sought to optimize, was the vector of scale factors (for each NMVOC species from each emission sector and for each grid) applied to the a priori emissions.  $x_{a}$  was a unit vector applied to the a priori NMVOC emission estimates.  $y$  was the vector of satellite-observed monthly mean VCDs of the targeted tracer (formaldehyde and/or glyoxal).  $F(x)$  was the vector of VCDs of the targeted tracer simulated by the forward model  $F$ .  $S_{0}$  was the a priori emission error covariance matrix, which we assumed to be diagonal. The observation error covariance matrix in Eq. (1),  $S_{0}$ , was difficult to quantify, as it included contributions not only from the satellite retrieval but also from the model representation of chemistry and transport. Zhu et al. (2016) and Chan Miller et al. (2017) compared vertical profiles of GEOS-Chem-simulated formaldehyde and glyoxal over the south

We first compared the formaldehyde and glyoxal VCDs simulated by the model (sampled at satellite overpass times) using the a priori emissions against those observed by GOME-2A and OMI, as well as against measurements at ground-based sites (Table S3). Figures 3 and 4 show the monthly mean formaldehyde VCDs observed by GOME-2A over China in 2007. Observed formaldehyde VCDs over China showed a distinct west-to-east gradient year-round, driven by the higher vegetation and population densities in eastern China. Observed formaldehyde VCDs were higher in summer than in winter due to a combination of stronger biogenic emissions and photochemistry during the warmer months. In spring, GOME-2A formaldehyde VCDs were high over southwest China and Southeast Asia, reflecting the occurrence of seasonal biomass burning. The highest formaldehyde VCDs were observed over the NCP in June, likely because of the large emissions from in-field crop residue burning.

Figure 3. Monthly mean formaldehyde VCDs over China from January to June. For each month, the panels from left to right show (a) formaldehyde VCDs observed by GOME-2A, (b) formaldehyde VCDs simulated by the model using a priori emission estimates, (c) the a posteriori formaldehyde VCDs from IE-1, (d) GOME-2A formaldehyde VCDs scaled by a factor of 1.7, and (e) the a posteriori formaldehyde VCDs from IE-3. All model results were sampled at GOME-2A overpass time. Also shown are ground-based MAX-DOAS measurements at 09:30 LT at Xianghe (monthly mean, circles) and Wuxi (bimonthly mean, upward triangles).

Figure 7. Monthly mean formaldehyde VCDs over China from January to June. For each month, the panels from left to right show (a) formaldehyde VCDs observed by OMI, (b) formaldehyde VCDs simulated by the model using a priori emission estimates, (c) the a posteriori formaldehyde VCDs from IE-2, and (d) the a posteriori formaldehyde VCDs from IE-4. All model results were sampled at OMI overpass time. Also shown are ground-based MAX-DOAS measurements at 13:30 LT at Xianghe (monthly mean, circles) and Wuxi (bimonthly mean, upward triangles).

Figure 9. Monthly mean glyoxal VCDs over China from January to June. For each month, the panels from left to right show (a) glyoxal VCDs observed by OMI, (b) glyoxal VCDs simulated by the model using a priori emission estimates, (c) the a posteriori glyoxal VCDs from IE-2, and (d) the a posteriori glyoxal VCDs from IE-4. All model results were sampled at OMI overpass time.

Figures 3 and 4 showed the a posteriori formaldehyde VCDs from the inversion experiment IE-3, which was constrained by the GOME-2A-observed formaldehyde VCDs scaled by a factor of 1.7. The a posteriori formaldehyde VCDs in IE-3 increased further over eastern China during the warmer months relative to IE-1, especially over the NCP and central China in summer. In December and January, the scaled-up GOME-2A observations over eastern China were still lower than the simulated formaldehyde VCDs using the a priori emissions, leading to a reduction in the a posteriori formaldehyde VCDs over eastern China (Table S5). Figure 2 shows that the top-down monthly emission estimates for all NMVOC species were lower than the a priori in November, December, January, and February and higher than the a priori for the warmer months. Consequently, although no observations of glyoxal were used as constraints in IE-3, the a posteriori glyoxal VCDs also decreased in winter and increased in summer, which was in better agreement with the GOME-2A observations (Figs. 5 and 6). This is consistent with our analyses in Sect. 3: the constraints exerted by the GOME-2A formaldehyde and glyoxal observations were consistent in winter and in summer, when the NMVOC emissions were dominated by anthropogenic and biogenic sources, respectively. However, IE-3 had almost no effects on the a posteriori glyoxal VCDs and the top-down emission estimates of anthropogenic glyoxal precursors in April and October. This demonstrated the necessity of glyoxal observations in constraining the emissions of NMVOC species that preferentially produced glyoxal, including most importantly the aromatics.

metadata.inferred_insight: The figure illustrates the effectiveness of inversion experiments IE-1 and IE-3 in refining glyoxal VCD estimates. It demonstrates improved summer predictions in line with observational data, underscoring the need for precise emissions constraints.

metadata.title: Figure 12. Validation of the model with flux measurements in China. Panels (a) and (b) show the performance of MEGAN with the default emission factors  N = 19 . Panels (c) and (d) show the performance of MEGAN with the emission factors derived from observations  N = 19 .
references: together with the PFT distribution. In order to understand the role of the emission factor, the flux measurements of isoprene and monoterpenes from the campaigns conducted from 2010 to 2016 in China (Bai et al., 2015, 2016, 2017) were collected and compared with model results in this study. The details of these campaigns are provided in Table 7, and the emission factors that were retrieved from the observations are also listed for these sites. Most samples were collected during the daytime every  $3\mathrm{h}$  according to the descriptions of the measurements (Bai et al., 2015, 2016, 2017); therefore, we averaged the model results from 08:00 to 20:00 in local time with a  $3\mathrm{h}$  interval for comparison. As shown in (a) and (b) of Fig. 12, the modeled fluxes of isoprene and monoterpenes with the default emission factors in this study did not capture the variability of the observations. The ME, MB and RMSE are 1.60, 1.59 and  $2.31\mathrm{mgm}^{-2}\mathrm{h}^{-1}$  for isoprene and 0.21, -0.003 and  $0.32\mathrm{mgm}^{-2}\mathrm{h}^{-1}$  for monoterpenes. When we adopted the emission factors retrieved from observations (Bai et al., 2015, 2016, 2017), the simulated isoprene and monoterpene fluxes showed relatively good consistence with the observations by using the same activity factor from the model  $(\gamma$  in Eq. 1) as shown in (c) and (b) of Fig. 12. The ME, MB and RMSE are 0.44, 0.41 and  $0.57\mathrm{mgm}^{-2}\mathrm{h}^{-1}$  for isoprene and 0.32, 0.14 and  $0.49\mathrm{mgm}^{-2}\mathrm{h}^{-1}$  for monoterpenes after adopting the observation-based emission factors, and the statistic parameters for isoprene simulation are largely improved. Although the MB and ME of monoterpene simulation increased, the simulated monoterpene flux shows better agreement with observations (Fig. 12). Therefore, it is clear that our calculation of activity factors is in a reasonable range, but the emission factor is the main source of uncertainties. The PFT-level emission factors used in this study from Guenther et al. (2012) represent the globally averaged emission factor for PFTs, and it is relatively easy to use them with the satellite PFT products. Therefore, most studies listed in Table 6 adopted the PFT or land use emission factors. Our validation showed that an accurate emission factor based on observations could largely improve the performance of MEGAN, but it also requires abundant efforts to conduct measurements. However, the measurements listed in Table 7 are still very limited for describing the spatial discrepancies of ecosystems in China, so we still used the default emission factors in MEGAN for our national-scale estimation. The estimations by Li et al. (2013, 2020) used the species-level emission factors and vegetation atlas of China for 2007 to describe the spatial distribution of BVOC emission potentials, and they concluded the reason why their estimations were far higher than other studies is the high emission factors they adopted. Therefore, the same validations by using canopy-scale BVOC flux measurements are also needed for these studies to validate and constrain the emission factors they used.

metadata.title: Fig. 4. The predictions of the STEAM2 model for total PM emission factor (legend, in units of  gKWh^{-1} ) as a function of engine load and fuel sulphur content.
references: In STEAM2, the PM emissions  $\left[\mathrm{g}\mathrm{kWh}^{-1}\right]$  are evaluated as the product of specific fuel-oil consumption and emission factors, where the relative SFOC is computed using Eq. (11). The variations of this emission factor have been graphically illustrated in Fig. 4. According to Lack et al. (2009) a clear correlation between fuel sulphur content and the emissions of organic carbon exists. It is not clear whether this is because of the changes in the type and consumption of lubricating oil, but this feature is not currently modeled by STEAM2, which assumes that OC emissions are independent of fuel sulphur content.

Abstract. A method is presented for the evaluation of the exhaust emissions of marine traffic, based on the messages provided by the Automatic Identification System (AIS), which enable the positioning of ship emissions with a high spatial resolution (typically a few tens of metres). The model also takes into account the detailed technical data of each individual vessel. The previously developed model was applicable for evaluating the emissions of  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ . This paper addresses a substantial extension of the modelling system, to allow also for the mass-based emissions of particulate matter (PM) and carbon monoxide (CO). The presented Ship Traffic Emissions Assessment Model (STEAM2) allows for the influences of accurate travel routes and ship speed, engine load, fuel sulphur content, multiengine setups, abatement methods and waves. We address in particular the modeling of the influence on the emissions of both engine load and the sulphur content of the fuel. The presented methodology can be used to evaluate the total PM emissions, and those of organic carbon, elemental carbon, ash and hydrated sulphate. We have evaluated the performance of the extended model against available experimental data on engine power, fuel consumption and the composition-resolved emissions of PM. We have also compared the annually averaged emission values with those of the corresponding EMEP inventory. As example results, the geographical distributions of the emissions of PM and CO are presented for the marine regions of the Baltic Sea surrounding the Danish Straits.

Fig. 3. The emission factor of the total PM, and for its chemical constituents as a function of fuel sulphur content (mass-based percentage), based on the data from the second IMO GHG study (Buhaug et al., 2009). Lines indicate the PM component emission factors in STEAM2. The emission factors of the total PM,  $\mathrm{SO}_4$  and  $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$  are linearly dependent on the fuel sulphur content. The scale used for total PM,  $\mathrm{SO}_4$  and associated  $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$  is different (left-hand axis) from the scale used for EC, OC and ash (right-hand axis).

where  $S$  is the fuel sulphur content in percentages and the emission coefficients for EC, OC and ash have been assumed to be independent of the sulphur content, but for OC an additional dependency on engine load is used. In Eq. (13c), the dimensionless constants are  $a = 1.024$ ,  $b = -47.660$  and  $c = 32.547$ , respectively. The  $\mathrm{EF_{OC}}$  emission as a function of engine load was fitted to the results of Agrawal et al. (2008a, b) and Petzold et al. (2010). A cut-off value at engine load of  $15\%$  was applied which constrains the OC emission factor to a constant value at very low engine loads. The amount of ash may change between different fuel grades, but this effect is neglected for now. The total PM emission factor (in  $\mathrm{g~kWh^{-1}}$ ) is assumed to be the sum of the above mentioned emission factors

The emission factor predictions by STEAM2 are compared with measurements available from literature in Fig. 11a–d. The engine loads and fuel sulphur contents in these studies are as follows:  $85\%$  and  $2.85\%$  (Agrawal et al., 2008b),  $84\%$  and  $1.90\%$  (Moldanova et al., 2009),  $85\%$  and  $2.21\%$  (Petzold et al., 2008), and  $57\%$  and  $3.01\%$  (Murphy et al., 2009). For simplicity, these studies are in the following referred to as AGR, MOL, PET and MUR. The engine load is within the commonly used operation range for the three first-mentioned studies, but it was substantially lower in MUR. The sulphur content of fuels varies from 1.9 to  $3.0\%$ .

In case of MUR and AGR, the ash emission factor was computed from the ash content of the fuel, whereas MOL and PET report directly measured values of ash. These ash emission factors are therefore not directly comparable with each other, and the MUR and AGR ash emission values are strictly speaking not comparable with the STEAM2 predictions. There may be processes during fuel combustion, which lead to changes in the amount of emitted ash. MOL reports the highest ash emissions, although the ash content of the fuel used by MOL is the lowest. In comparison with PET, the STEAM2 ash emission factors are in a good agreement. The ash emissions in principle depend on the ash content of the fuel, but this is not taken into account in the model. However, one cannot conclude based on the above comparison of predictions and data that this would be a significant impact. In regional scale studies of ship emissions, fuel sulphur content of each vessel is not known and assumptions have to be

Data from the EMEP can be compared with the predictions of the STEAM and STEAM2 models. According to STEAM2, the predicted levels of  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  were  $8.6 - 17.4\%$  higher than those of EMEP in 2006-2009. For  $\mathrm{SO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  emissions, the STEAM2 predictions were  $24.2\%$ ,  $21.3\%$  and  $8.9\%$  lower than those by EMEP in 2006-2008, but  $1.9\%$  higher in 2009. The temporal trend in the EMEP data for the  $\mathrm{SO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  emissions from 2006 to 2007 exhibits a decrease that is steeper compared with that predicted by STEAM2; both inventories include the effect of SECA rules for the marine fuel sulphur content. For CO and PM, the STEAM2 predictions are higher than those of EMEP; both the annually averaged emissions and their geographical distribution are different. The inclusion of the load dependency of the emission factors (in STEAM2) results in relatively higher emissions in congested marine areas, in contrast to using a fixed emission factor that leads to linearly increasing emissions as a function of instantaneous engine power.

metadata.title: Fig. 1. The frequency of sulfur and ash contents in coal and that of removal efficiencies of  SO_2  and  NO_X  for Jiangsu's power plants and other industrial boilers, based on plant-by-plant investigation. The plant numbers are indicated in the parentheses.
metadata.supplement: (a) For power plants;(b) For other industrial boilers;(c) For power plants;(d) For other industrial boilers
references: To quantify the uncertainties of regional (provincial and city) inventories, Monte Carlo framework was improved by incorporating plant-specific information and then applied at each plant for point sources. As the energy consumption data of power sector were compiled and crosschecked based on multiple data sources (Zhou et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2015), we assumed that the uncertainty would be smaller than other sources and we tentatively set the CV at  $10\%$  for each individual plant. For point sources of other categories and area sources, in contrast, the CVs of activity data were set at  $20\%$ , the same as the national inventory. For emission factors, we divided the parameters into two types, i.e., the common ones and plant-specific ones. The common parameters indicated those that keep stable or change slightly for certain technology/fuel type, e.g., the sulfur and ash release ratio of bituminous pulverized combustion. The PDFs were determined following Zhao et al. (2011, 2013), with the most recent measurement data incorporated. The plant-specific parameters indicated those that are independent and might vary largely between different plants, e.g., the  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  removal efficiencies of flue gas desulfurization (FGD) and selective catalytic reduction (SCR) systems, respectively, and the sulfur and ash contents of fossil fuels. Available information was collected and comprehensively compiled to generate the PDFs for those parameters. Details are discussed in the following section.

Plant-specific parameters were applied mainly in uncertainty analysis of point sources in regional inventories (for area sources and point sources without detailed information, common parameters were applied instead). For sources related to fossil fuel combustion (e.g., power plants, industrial boilers/kilns, and industrial processes that contain coal combustion), the PDFs of sulfur content, ash content, and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{NO_X}$  removal efficiencies of corresponding air pollutant control devices (APCDs) were determined for each plant. Fig. 1a and b shows the frequencies of sulfur and ash contents for Jiangsu's power plants and other industrial boilers. The average sulfur content was lower for coals used for power generation than those for other boilers. Normal distributions were assumed for sulfur and ash contents of coal burned for individual plant. Standard deviations of those distributions were determined for each plant with following steps. First, standard deviation for the whole sector was determined through normal distribution fitting with all the data samples within the sector. The difference between sulfur/ash content of single plant and average of the whole sector was then calculated for each plant. The larger value obtained from above two steps was selected as the standard deviation for single plant.

Fig. 1c and d shows the frequencies of removal efficiencies of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{NO_X}$  for Jiangsu's power and other industrial plants. For both source categories,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  control systems were more widely applied than  $\mathrm{NO_X}$  control, while the average  $\mathrm{NO_X}$  removal efficiency based on limited data samples was higher than that of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  for other industrial boilers. Triangular distribution was assumed for the  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  removal efficiency for each plant. Clear difference was found between sectors. In 2012,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  control technologies (including wetFGD and other simple technologies) were applied at  $90\%$  of Jiangsu's coal-fired power units, with the average removal efficiency calculated at  $68\%$ . For industrial boilers and kilns, in contrast,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  control was applied only  $18\%$  of the plants and the average removal rate was  $42\%$ . Thus the ranges of triangular distributions differed between source categories. For power sector, the ranges of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  removal rates for FGD systems and other technologies (e.g., circulating fluidized bed, CFB) were  $50 - 98\%$  and  $10 - 85\%$ , respectively, according to the investigated data. For other industrial boilers and kilns, similarly, the ranges were determined at  $50 - 98\%$ ,  $30 - 85\%$ ,  $10 - 60\%$ , and  $10 - 85\%$  for wet-FGD, dry-FGD, CFB and other technologies, respectively. Besides coal combustion, FGD systems were also applied for other industrial processes. Relatively high removal efficiencies were found for sintering in iron & steel production, and a triangular distribution ranged  $50 - 98\%$  was assumed for individual plant. For other processes including production of ammonia,

Table 4 summarized the 1st and 2nd ranking parameters contributing most to the uncertainties of Jiangsu's and Nanjing's emissions in the provincial and city-level inventory by industrial source category, respectively. There were differences in the key parameter identification for the province in the national and provincial inventory. For  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  from power sector, as an example, the removal efficiencies of FGD for given individual plants were identified as the most significant parameters to the uncertainty, with very limited contribution to variance. Similarly, the uncertainty of emissions from other industrial combustions was estimated to be mostly affected by the sulfur content and FGD removal rate of one single plant. metadata.source_md: Estimation of emissions from biomass burning in Ch.md
metadata.save_path: C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Estimation of emissions from biomass burning in Ch\figure_2.jpg
metadata.title: Figure 2. Spatial distribution of  CO_{2}  emissions (tonne) from each land-cover type (excluding small islands in the South China Sea).
references: Nationwide spatial patterns of  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions from four sources are shown in Fig. 2 (biomass fire emissions from the small islands in the South China Sea are not included). Forest and grassland fire emissions were mainly distributed in northeastern China and southern China. Dense vegetative covers in the Yunnan-Guizhou Plateau, Inner Mongolian Plateau, Daxing'anling, Xiaoxing'anling, and the southeast hills greatly contribute to fire events. Cropland fire emissions were concentrated in the three great plains of China, namely the Northeast China Plain, the North China Plain, and the Middle-Lower Yangtze Plain. Because of high crop production in these areas, large quantities of agricultural residues were burned in fields during the short period following the harvest season. In addition, due to snowmelt in the Tian Shan, there are many oases located at the foot of the mountain range in Xinjiang Province. These oases are suitable for growing crops such as wheat and maize (Zhou et al., 2017). Therefore, crop fires emissions in Xinjiang province were higher than those in other northwestern provinces. Compared with other fire types, emissions from shrubland fire were negligible and the high emissions were concentrated in Guangdong and Yunnan provinces.

In this study, we used fire radiative power (FRP) data derived from the MODIS active fire products to calculate emissions of 11 pollutants from biomass burning in China (excluding fires occurring on the small islands in the South China Sea) for the period of 2003-2017. The spatiotemporal distribution of emissions from four biomass burning types (forest, grassland, cropland, and shrubland fires) were studied in detail. A daily gridded  $1\mathrm{km}$  emission inventory of biomass burning was established; this inventory could meet the requirements of global and regional air quality simulations.

Monthly mean T/A ratios were calculated for each type of biomass burning. Different combustion characteristics of fuel types could be reflected by specific T/A ratios. As shown in Fig. S1 in the Supplement (excluding small islands in the South China Sea), China is divided into six temperature zones (tropical zone, subtropical zone, warm-temperate zone, middle-temperate zone, cold-temperate zone, and the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau). Because the dominant crop types vary greatly among temperature zones, we calculated T/A ratios for each zone separately. Using respective T/A ratios to calculate factors required in Eq. (1), the FRP diurnal cycle was parameterized for each zone and harvest season, which could reflect specific combustion characteristics of different straw types.

The GlobeLand30 dataset maps global land cover at  $30\mathrm{m}$  spatial resolution in two base years (2000 and 2010) (Chen et al., 2017b), as shown in Fig. S2 (small islands in the South China Sea are not included). GlobeLand30 data are generated by multispectral images derived from Landsat TM,  $\mathrm{ETM + }$ , and the Chinese environmental disaster alleviation satellite Huanjing-1A (HJ-1). The result of the accuracy assessment shows that the overall accuracy of GlobeLand30 reaches  $83.5\%$ . GlobeLand30 dataset consists of 10 land-cover types, namely cultivated land, forest, grassland, shrubland, wetland, water bodies, tundra, artificial surfaces, bare land, and permanent snow and ice. In this study, the land-cover types are characterized by GlobeLand30-2000 for years 2003-2005 and GlobeLand30-2010 for years 2006-2017. We combined the land-cover map of China and the latitude and longitude data of fire count in MOD14/MYD14 to determine the biomass fuel types. For instance, if a fire count was located in a cropland area, it was considered a crop residue burning event.
metadata.concise_summary: The figure illustrates the spatial distribution of CO2 emissions from different land-cover types in China, excluding small islands in the South China Sea.
metadata.inferred_insight: The figure highlights areas with significant biomass fire emissions, correlating with land-cover types and geographic regions, to understand spatial emission patterns for regional air quality analysis.

metadata.title: Fig. 2. Emission factors of  PM_2.5 emitted from 10 tested vehicles.
references: The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of six gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 2. There was only one China III gasoline vehicle and its  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $1.675\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Three gasoline vehicles with China IV emission standard had an average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of  $1.452\mathrm{mg / km}$ . The average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of other two China V gasoline vehicles was  $0.783\mathrm{mg / km}$ . The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of gasoline vehicles decreased gradually with the increasing emission standards. Four diesel vehicles were tested in this experiment as well. Only one China III diesel vehicle was tested and its  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $33.095\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Two diesel vehicles with China IV emission standard had an average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of  $15.134\mathrm{mg / km}$ . There was only one China V diesel vehicle and its  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor was  $2.994\mathrm{mg / km}$ . As the emission standards rose up from China III to China V, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles decreased by more than  $90\%$ . As emission standards became stricter, the emission factor of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  gradually decreased because vehicles with higher emission standards usually had better exhaust control devices. Meanwhile, vehicles with higher emission standards were often younger than vehicles with lower emission standards (Zhang et al., 2019a, b). Under the same emission standards, the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of diesel vehicles were obviously higher than gasoline vehicles, because the molecules that made up diesel were usually larger than gasoline (Distillation range of gasoline and diesel see Table s5.), and the engines could not be evenly mixed with air during

Vehicle emission is an important contributor to urban air pollution with the increasing number of motor vehicles. Ten typical vehicles were selected in Wuhan to study the emissions of fine particular matters  $\mathrm{(PM_{2.5})}$  and associated chemical components by on-road tests through a Portable Emission Monitoring System (PEMS). The emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and the compositions of it from different types of vehicle were obtained. Results showed that the average emission factors of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline and diesel vehicles were 1.266 and  $16.589\mathrm{mg}/$  km. As the emission standard of vehicles increased from China III to China V,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor gradually decreased from 17.385 to  $1.520~\mathrm{mg / km}$ . Emission rate of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was  $0.0384~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under low speed, and it increased to 0.0775 and  $0.0964~\mathrm{mg / s}$  under the medium and high speeds. The ratio of organic carbon versus elemental carbon (OC/EC) in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles was 6.89, which was greater than that of diesel vehicles as 3.12. Because gasoline was made of small molecules and the compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder. The top four water-soluble ions with high emission factors were  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2 - }$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2 + }$ , and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , while K, Na, Ca and Mg had a larger emission factors in the 21 tested inorganic elements. These water-soluble ions and inorganic elements mainly came from the oil burning, fuel additives and engines wear. Results of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission characteristics would help to improve the air quality in Wuhan.

As the pollution of vehicle emission becomes a prominent social problem, the monitoring methods of vehicle emissions are developed and improved continuously (Giechaskiel et al., 2014; Xue et al., 2018; Farwick et al., 2019). These methods mainly include remote sensing detection, vehicle bench test, tunnel test and portable vehicle emission measurement (Giechaskiel et al., 2018; Mahesh et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Remote sensing is an effective and economic tool to monitor vehicle emissions, while it is accurate in identifying gasoline vehicles but not diesel ones (Huang et al., 2018). And the chemical composition of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  can not be obtained by remote sensing (Smit et al., 2019). Myung et al. (2009) carried out bench tests on light motor vehicles and found that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions of using liquefied petroleum gas and biodiesel were much lower than those of gasoline and diesel. Carbonaceous components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles could be studied through a bench test, and it found that the driving speed directly affected the EC emissions (Fraser et al., 2002). However, the fluence of actual driving conditions on vehicle emissions could not be reflected (Pouresmaeili et al., 2018). Sánchez et al. (2019) studied the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors of both light and heavy vehicles through tunnel experiments, and the emission factors of heavy vehicles tended to be much higher than those of light vehicles. Tunnel experiments were also conducted by Hao et al. (2019) to study their characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from motor vehicles in Xi'an, China, and the results showed that OC accounted for  $34.10\%$  of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . Since tunnels in different locations have different speed limits, and it is difficult to cover both high-speed and real-world driving conditions in some tunnels (Huang et al., 2017; Smit et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020a, b). Huang et al. (2013) used PEMS to detect the concentrations of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from buses, and cars under actual driving conditions, and they found that concentrations of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  increased with the driving speed. PEMS was also used to analyze the chemical composition of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from heavy diesel trucks, and it was found that EC and OC were the major components of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (Zhang et al., 2015). Based on the results of previous researches, the conclusions drawn from various cities were obviously different due to their geographical locations, climates, populations, etc. Wuhan is located in the central of China, and it has a large population and a large number of motor vehicles with high humidity climate (Yang et al., 2019). The effect of humidity on some gaseous pollutants (such as CO and NOx) emitted from vehicles has been studied (Dolly et al., 2020), while few research were carried out on the  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and its chemical components. Therefore, it was necessary to carry out the research on vehicle emissions of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in Wuhan, China.

from vehicles became outstanding in Wuhan. In order to find out the characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations and associated chemical components from on-road emissions in urban areas in Wuhan, which was located in the central and southern China and the climates were high temperature and humidity, ten typical motor vehicles were selected to carry out this research.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factors and emission concentration characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from motor vehicles under different emission standards at different operating speeds were performed. The characteristics of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  components, including carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements, were detected as well.  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  composition spectrum of vehicle emission in Wuhan would be finally obtained. Results of this study would help formulating measures for the reduction of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emissions from vehicles in Wuhan, and provide a reference for  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  pollution control of motor vehicles in similar types.

$\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles were collected by the SEMTECH-PFS sub-module. SEMTECH-PFS sub-module had three channels for membrane sampling, where three sampling membranes with a diameter of  $47\mathrm{mm}$  were located. These three channels could not be used to collect  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  at the same time. When one channel was ventilated and sampling, the other two channels were closed. Computer was used to decide the channel of the airflow. Before sampling started, a quartz filter (MK360, Munktel, Sweden) was placed on Channel 1, a Teflon filter (R2PJ047, PALL, USA) was placed on Channel 2 and another quartz filter was placed on Chanel 3. The quartz filter membrane on Chanel 1 was mainly used for analyzing organic carbon, elemental carbon and water-soluble ions. The Teflon filter membrane on Chanel 2 was used for analyzing elements. The quartz filter membrane on Chanel 3 was used as the blank sample.

Carbonaceous aerosols consisted of organic carbon (OC), elemental carbon (EC), and a small amount of carbonate. Since carbonates were very small part in atmospheric particles and the proportion of carbonates was less than  $5\%$  of total carbon (TC), they would not be discussed in this study. The DRI thermal carbon analyzer (IMPROVE-A, DRI, USA) was used to detect the concentration of OC and EC, when  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  sample was collected without the pretreatment after equilibrium weighing. Several water-soluble ions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles were analyzed, which included  $\mathrm{K}^{+}$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ,  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ,  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ,  $\mathrm{NH}_4^+$ ,  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  and  $\mathrm{F}^-$ . Ion chromatograph (IC) was used to analyze the water-soluble ions that collected on the filter. 21 crustal elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). The mass concentration of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from vehicle emissions could be obtained through the CPM sub-module in SEMTECH-ECOSTAR. Combining the exhaust gas flow data recorded by the exhaust gas flow meter and the vehicle driving conditions data recorded by the GPS, the emission rate under the actual driving conditions could be calculated. According to the driving speed, the driving conditions of the tested vehicles were divided into eight speed segments: idle speed (0 km/h), 0–10 km/h, 0–20 km/h, 20–30 km/h, 30–40 km/h, 40–50 km/h, 50–60 km/h and >60 km/h. The average  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission rates of the tested vehicles in all the eight speed segments were calculated, and the results were shown in Fig. 3.
Table 2 Emission factors of carbonaceous components in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from vehicles.

In Table 2, the average emission factors of TC for China III, IV and V gasoline vehicles were 1086.15, 1054.06 and  $397.94~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. When compared to China III vehicles, the TC emission factors of China IV and V vehicles decreased by  $2.95\%$  and  $63.36\%$ . Meanwhile, it could be found that the OC emission factors of the tested gasoline vehicles were 8.68 times larger than those of EC except Vehicle 1#. TC emission factor of diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. To find out the TC emission characteristics of diesel vehicles with different emission standards, the average emission factors of TC for China III, IV and V vehicles were 25,973.93, 11,745.64 and  $1735.88~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. When compared to China III diesel vehicles, the TC emission factors of China IV and V vehicles decreased by  $54.78\%$  and  $93.32\%$ . OC emission factors of diesel vehicles were also 1.06 times larger than EC emission factors, which was similar to the OC and EC emission characteristics of gasoline vehicles.

The ratio of OC/EC was an important parameter in the carbonaceous components of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , which was often used to determine the source of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ . The OC/EC ratios of carbonaceous compositions from the 10 tested vehicles were shown in Fig. 4, and the average OC/EC ratios of the vehicles with different emission standards were figured out as well. The OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was higher than 3.0 except for Vehicle 1#. It meant there were many organic components in the carbonaceous component, which was consistent with the results of some recent studies (James et al., 2002). The OC/EC ratio of Vehicle 1# was only 0.3, which deviated from the normal value of gasoline vehicle emissions. Because Vehicle 1# was a taxi with both gasoline and natural gas functions, and the engine used a mixture of gasoline and natural gas for a long time. It used gasoline fuel during the test, and insufficient combustion of gasoline in the engine caused an increase in the number of black carbon particles, which led to a small ratio of OC/EC. The average OC/EC ratio of China IV Vehicles was 10.2, and it decreased to 5.3 in China V gasoline vehicles. This indicated the improvement of emission standards could reduce the percentage of OC in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from gasoline vehicles. For diesel vehicles, the average OC/EC ratios of China III, IV and V vehicles were 1.5, 3.0, and 4.9, respectively. The OC/EC ratio of diesel vehicles turned to decrease as the emission standard rose up. It could also be seen from Fig. 4 that the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was generally greater than that of diesel vehicles, which was mainly because diesel was more likely to form unburned soot particles during the combustion process (Shen et al., 2021). There were two main differences between gasoline vehicles and diesel vehicles, fuel composition and engine structure. Gasoline was made of small molecules and was easy to mix evenly with air. After the spark plug discharged ignition, the fuel burned rapidly. The compression ratio of the gasoline engine was relatively low, and a large amount of OC remained in the area where the

The order of the water-soluble ion emission factors in the China III gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-} > \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - } > \mathrm{Ca}^{2 + } > \mathrm{Na}^{+} > \mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-} > \mathrm{Mg}^{2 + } > \mathrm{K}^{+}>$ $\mathrm{F}^{-} > \mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$  as shown in Fig. 5a. The emission factors of  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\mathrm{Ca^{2 + }}$  in the top three positions were  $123.50~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $73.51~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $62.65~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three emission factors of the water-soluble ions in China IV gasoline vehicles were  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ and  $\mathrm{Ca^{2 + }}$ , which were  $56.71~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $26.84~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $20.12~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three water-soluble ions in China V gasoline vehicles were  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ , and their emission factors were  $52.28~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.91~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $21.70~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. With the exception of  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$ , the emission factors of the tested water-soluble ions decreased gradually as the emission standard increased, which was consistent with the trend of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  changing with emission standards. As could be seen from Fig. 5b that the order of the water-soluble ions emission factors in China III diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-} > \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - } > \mathrm{Mg}^{2 + } > \mathrm{Na}^{+} > \mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-} > \mathrm{Ca}^{2 + } > \mathrm{K}^{+}>$ $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+} > \mathrm{F}^{-}$ . The average emission factors of  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$ , and  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + }$  in the top three were  $1194.27~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $514.26~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $359.95~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ . The top three emission factors of the water-soluble ions in China IV diesel vehicles were  $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ , which were  $844.52~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $487.50~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $456.73~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The top three water-soluble ions in China V diesel vehicles were  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2 - }$  and  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2 + }$ , and their emission factors were  $167.63~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $110.68~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $71.82~\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.

From the perspective of changes in emission standards, the emission factors of  $\mathrm{Cl}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$  and  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  decreased as the emission standards increased. While the emission factors of  $\mathrm{NH}_4^+$ ,  $\mathrm{F}^-$ ,  $\mathrm{Na}^+$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  and  $\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  increased to the top in China IV diesel vehicles. The percentages of  $\mathrm{Na}^+$ ,  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ ,  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$  and  $\mathrm{NO}_3^-$  in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  from gasoline vehicles were  $2.258\%$ ,  $1.788\%$ ,  $0.773\%$ ,  $2.186\%$  and  $0.623\%$ , respectively. While their percentages from diesel vehicles were  $2.683\%$ ,  $0.789\%$ ,  $1.782\%$ ,  $2.918\%$  and  $0.857\%$ , respectively. The percentage of water-soluble ions in this study (13.12% in gasoline vehicles and 14.53% in diesel vehicles) were higher than  $10\%$  reported by Zhang et al. (2015), whose research was conducted in Beijing, a relatively dry city. However, the kinds of top water-soluble ions and the orders of them were similar to this study. Thus, humidity helped increase the percentage of water-soluble ions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and still keep the order of top water-soluble ions. Cheng et al. The  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from 10 typical vehicles were analyzed by ICP-MS, and 21 inorganic elements including Na, Mg, K, Ca, V, Cr, Mn, Fe, Co, Ni, Cu, Zn, Ga, As, Rb, Sr, Ag, Cd, Cs, Ba and Pb were detected. The emission factors of inorganic elements under different emission standards for gasoline vehicles were shown in Fig. 6a and diesel vehicles were shown in Fig. 6b.

It could be seen from Fig. 6a that the order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Cu}$ . Their emission factors were  $2.051\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.922\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.827\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.783\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.142\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.048\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.011\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.009\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $3.423\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.740\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.272\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.534\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.136\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.100\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.059\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.034\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.022\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ . The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China V gasoline vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $1.047\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.852\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.689\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.202\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.065\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.027\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.026\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.015\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.005\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ .

The emission factors of all the tested inorganic elements from diesel vehicles were larger than gasoline vehicles when compared Fig. 6b with Fig. 6a, which was consistent with the fact that  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emission factor of diesel vehicles was larger than gasoline vehicles. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China III diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ . Their emission factors were  $100.10\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $30.65\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $19.26\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $6.84\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.72\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.05\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.83\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.75\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.64\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top nine emission factors for China IV diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Mg} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Zn} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb}$ , which was same as China III. Their emission factors were  $81.00\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $28.73\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $16.80\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $4.06\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $3.96\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.62\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.08\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.71\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.69\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. The order of inorganic elements with the top eight emission factors for China V diesel vehicles was  $\mathrm{K} > \mathrm{Na} > \mathrm{Ca} > \mathrm{Sr} > \mathrm{Fe} > \mathrm{Cr} > \mathrm{Pb} > \mathrm{Zn}$ . Their emission factors were  $74.12\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.85\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $7.32\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $2.36\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $1.20\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.53\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ ,  $0.40\mu \mathrm{g / km}$  and  $0.29\mu \mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively.

It could be found out from Fig. 6 that the emission factors of Na, Mg, K, and Ca were obviously larger than those of other inorganic elements for both gasoline and diesel vehicles. This mainly because the fuel was prepared with additives which contained Na, Mg, K, while Ca. Fe, Zn, Sr, Cr, Mn, Pb and some other inorganic elements had lower emission factors, because these mainly came from a small amount of additives, catalysts and partly from the wear during engine operation. The percentages of inorganic elements were less than  $1.0\%$  both in gasoline and diesel vehicles, which was much smaller than  $27.73\%$  that reported by

The data of Table 3 showed that OC and EC were the two types of substances that accounted for the largest proportion of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  in vehicle emissions. The proportion of OC in gasoline and diesel vehicles were  $53.926\%$  and  $51.151\%$ , respectively. The proportion of EC in diesel vehicles (25.169%) was nearly twice as much as gasoline vehicles (13.068%), which indicated more EC components were emitted from diesel vehicles than gasoline vehicles.  $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  and  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  were the water-soluble ions with the top five proportions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. The proportion of  $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$  in gasoline vehicles was more than twice of diesel vehicles, while the proportion of  $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$  in diesel vehicles was more than twice of gasoline vehicles. These might be because the additives in gasoline and diesel vehicles were different. The additives in gasoline vehicles might contain more Ca, while additives in diesel vehicles might contain more Mg. K, Na, Ca, Mg, Sr, Fe, Zn and Pb were the inorganic elements with higher proportions in  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  emitted from the tested vehicles. K, Na, Ca, and Mg were mainly derived from fuel additives, while Sr, Fe, Zn and Pb were mainly derived from the wear and tear of vehicles engines.

Laboratory analysis on collected filter membranes was carried out to study the characteristics of carbonaceous components, water-soluble ions and inorganic elements. Emission factor of carbonaceous components emitted from diesel vehicles was 9.15 times higher than that of gasoline vehicles. However, the OC/EC ratio of gasoline vehicles was over twice of diesel vehicles. The compression ratio of gasoline engine was relatively low, and some OC remained in the area where the ignition failed in the cylinder.

metadata.title: Fig. 3. reduction effects of the WCHP project on major pollutants in eastern and western region.
references: In order to intuitively illustrate the effects of the WCHP project on air pollutants, we separately calculated percentage reduction effects of this policy in the eastern and western regions (Fig. 3). In the eastern region, decreasing percentages of AQI,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , and  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  were  $15.51\%$ ,  $14.72\%$ ,  $71.08\%$ , and  $31.22\%$ , respectively. In the western region, corresponding decreasing percentages were  $25.02\%$ ,  $22.25\%$ ,  $65.43\%$ , and  $36.49\%$ , respectively. Decreases of most

Coal used for heating rural households poses a severe threat to ambient air pollution and human health. In 2017, China implemented the Winter Clean Heating Pilot (WCHP) project, which was designed to convert energy from "coal-to-gas/electricity". However, the impact of the WCHP project and its associated dynamic changes have not yet been well quantified. Based on daily monitored panel data covering 35 pilot cities, we conducted a difference-in-differences (DID) model to identify whether or not the WCHP project is effective in maintaining air quality. Results showed that AQI,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ , and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions decreased by  $20.4\%$ ,  $18.59\%$ ,  $34.1\%$ , and  $68.4\%$ , respectively, and, except for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , these pollutant reductions were greater in Western China than in Eastern China. Furthermore, when certain variables were controlled, such as weather, holidays, GDP, the ratio between secondary industries, income levels, and environmental expenditures, these results were also robust. Parallel trends and placebo tests were additionally used to confirm the reliability of the DID model. The implementation of energy transition could gain the synergy between emission reduction and air quality improvement. Policy recommendations were finally made on rural-scale energy transition, including reforming the cost of electricity/gas through advanced technology and subsidies to account for regional inequality.

Table 4 provides the pollutant reduction effects under the WCHP project using the DID model. As a weighted-average indicator of primary air pollutants, AQI can objectively represent the degree of air pollution [40].  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  had the highest percentage increment derived from scattered coal combustion during winter heating periods.  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{NO_x}$  were the main components of acid rain. Hence, we selected AQI,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , and  $\mathrm{NO_x}$  as explanatory variables. Results showed that AQI significantly increased (42.79) during periods when heating was employed, while the WCHP project decreased AQI by 8.72, offsetting approximately  $20.38\%$  of the negative impacts caused by winter heating. Likewise,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations decreased by  $18.59\%$  due to the WCHP project. This finding indicated that the WCHP project has had a significant reduction effect on air pollution during winter heating periods, particularly for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , since  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions decreased significantly by  $68.36\%$ . However, the reduction effect on  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  remained far from the percentage released by the official feedback report (i.e.,  $33.7\%$ ). These results indicated that the net effect was smaller than the

pollutants in the western region were greater than that in the eastern region, except for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ . Coal-fired power generation and scattered coal combustion in the eastern region led to extremely high  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  emissions, while abundant clean energy sources (i.e., wind power, natural gas, and geothermal power) in the western region decreased the dependence of coal to some extent. Thus, the WCHP project was more effective in the eastern region.

winter heating increased  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_2$ , and  $\mathrm{CO}$  concentrations by  $41.7\%$ ,  $26.7\%$ ,  $19.2\%$ ,  $8.8\%$ , and  $24.4\%$ , respectively. Concentrations of AQI,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  decreased by  $20.4\%$ ,  $18.59\%$ ,  $34.1\%$ , and  $68.4\%$ , respectively. This indicated that the WCHP project can effectively reduce pollutant emissions, and the robustness test verified the reliability of our findings. Except for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , the reduction effects in Western China were greater than those in Eastern China. Moreover, the WCHP project led to significant improvements in the air quality of the pilot cities. Based on our findings on air quality improvements derived from the energy transition initiative, we proposed several policy recommendations.

metadata.title: Fig. 1. Scatter plots between the measured VOC levels and initial levels derived from a time-resolved approach (see Section 2.2 for details). The error bars represent the 95th confidence intervals of the mean values.
references: Fig. 1 shows the scatter plot between the measured and initial VOC concentrations. The ratios of the initial to measured levels for alkanes, alkenes, and aromatics were  $1.13 \pm 0.12$  (mean  $\pm$  standard deviation),  $7.90 \pm 10.3$ , and  $2.58 \pm 2.37$ , respectively. Large standard deviations of alkenes and aromatics reflected diversity in the reactivity of some species of them. It also suggested that species with higher react rates with OH radical such as isoprene ( $k_{\mathrm{OH}} = 100 \times 10^{12} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$  molecule  $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ ), trans-2-pentene ( $k_{\mathrm{OH}} = 67 \times 10^{12} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$  molecule  $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ ), and styrene ( $k_{\mathrm{OH}} = 58 \times 10^{12} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$  molecule  $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ ) were more impacted by photochemical processes.

Fig. 8. Scatter plots between the source profiles derived from the measured and initial VOC levels. The error bars represent the standard deviations of the bootstrap source profiles  $(\mathrm{N} = 100)$ . The color solid lines represent the linear regression results and the filled ribbons represent the  $95\%$  confidence intervals of fitting. The black solid lines represent the 1:1 line.

metadata.title: Fig. 4. Source profiles of five-factor solution derived from the PMF modelling using the initial dataset. The box and whisker plots are constructed according to the 25-75th and 1-99th percentiles of the bootstrap results  N = 100 . The squares represent the median values of bootstrap. SU: solvent usage, CS: combustion sources, VE: vehicle emissions, GE: gasoline evaporation, IN: industrial sources.
references: Fig. 4 shows the source profiles derived from the PMF bootstrap results. The diurnal variations of each source are shown in Fig. 5. The Pearson correlations between VOC source contributions, meteorological parameters, and other air pollutants were analyzed to support the source identification results (Table S5).

results by the PMF modelling. The measured and initial datasets were both introduced into the PMF model and the source profiles (Fig. 4 and Fig. S3) from two datasets were compared as shown in Fig. 8. The source profiles of gasoline evaporation, solvent usage, combustion sources, and industrial sources derived from two different datasets were in line with each other, with Pearson coefficient  $(r^2)$  of 0.99, 0.85, 0.68, and 0.40, respectively. However, a great discrepancy was found for vehicle emissions. The PMF algorithms aim to find the profiles and contributions for a given number of factors that have the minimum weighed residues of the input dataset. If the importance of atmospheric photochemical processes exceeds the differences of emission profiles from various sources, the factors in PMF can still be identified according to the different degrees of photochemical processing (Yuan et al., 2012). The source profiles derived from the two datasets suggested that the importance of photochemistry in the atmosphere exceeded the differences in the VOC compositions of vehicle emissions. Compared to the source apportionment from the measured dataset, the contributions of solvent usage, gasoline evaporation, and industrial sources to total VOC mixing ratio derived from the initial dataset were higher, while vehicle emissions and combustion sources were lower (Fig. S4). The differences suggested that the VOC decay should be considered before source apportionment using the receptor model.
metadata.concise_summary: The figure displays box plots of source profiles from PMF modelling derived from an initial dataset, highlighting median values and percentile ranges for five factors: solvent usage, combustion sources, vehicle emissions, gasoline evaporation, and industrial sources.
metadata.inferred_insight: The figure provides insight into the different source contributions to VOCs, with significant variation noted between emissions profiles and the influence of atmospheric photochemistry on VOC composition and source identification.

metadata.title: Figure 2. The comparison of monthly anomaly of downward shortwave (DSW) radiation (a) and  2m  temperature  T2  (b) for model simulation and in situ observation, and the filled areas present the standard deviations among 98 sites for DSW and 697 sites for  T2 .
references: We also compared the monthly anomalies of DSW and  $T2$  from the model simulation and observation to validate the interannual variability of meteorological fields simulated by WRF. As shown in Fig. 2, the results indicate that the model accurately reproduced the interannual variability of DSW and  $T2$ , and the correlation coefficients of DSW and  $T2$  anomaly between the simulation and observation reached 0.77 and 0.88, respectively. The trends in growing-season-averaged  $T2$  and DSW from model results as well as in situ measurements are presented in Fig. 3. The model and the in

Since light and temperature conditions are the main environmental drivers of BVOC emission (Guenther et al., 1993; Sakulyanontvittaya et al., 2008), we assessed the reliability of the WRF-simulated DSW and  $2\mathrm{m}$  temperature  $(T2)$  using in situ observations from 98 radiation observation sites and 697 meteorology observation sites in China. The in situ observations are from the National Meteorological Information Center (http://data.cma.cn/, last access: 25 March 2021). We converted the hourly model outputs and daily observations to monthly averaged values from 2001 to 2016 for comparison. For DSW, the average mean bias (MB), mean error (ME) and root-mean-square error (RMSE) are 40.37 ( $\pm$ 20.81), 43.55 ( $\pm$ 17.52) and 49.79 ( $\pm$ 17.70) W m $^{-2}$  for 98 studied sites. The overestimation of DSW simulation is a common issue in multiple simulation studies and may be induced by the lack of physical processes for aerosol radiation effect (Wang et al., 2011; Situ et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2018) and misrepresentation of the radiative effect of the sub-grid-scale cumulus cloud (Ruiz-Arias et al., 2016). For  $T2$ , the average MB, ME and RMSE are  $-1.19$  ( $\pm$ 2.87), 2.40 ( $\pm$ 2.14) and 2.65 ( $\pm$ 2.11) °C among 697 sites over China. The regional-scale validation was also conducted in five main regions (Fig. S1) by comparing the averaged values of the observation and the simulation among the sites in the regions we defined, and the results (Figs. S2 and S3) and statistical parameters (Tables S5 and S6) can be found in the Supplement.

references: Figure 5. The horizontal distributions of isoprene, monoterpenes, sesquiterpenes and total BVOCs emissions of China in 2001 are shown in panels (a), (g), (m) and (s), respectively. The rest of the rows present the changing trend of isoprene (b-f), monoterpenes (h-l), sesquiterpenes (n-r) and total BVOCs (t-x) in S1, S2, S3, S4 and S5, respectively. the estimation of monoterpene emission is about  $23.5\%$  and  $31.3\%$  higher than that from CAMS-GLOB-BIO v3.1 and v1.1, respectively. We further compared our results with two versions of CAMS-GLOB-BIO inventories. Figures 10 and 11 present the trends of isoprene emission and monoterpene emission, respectively, from S1 and S3 in this study and the CAMS-GLOB-BIO inventory v1.1 and v3.1 from 2001-2016. As shown in Figs. 10 and 11, S3 shows similar spatial patterns and magnitude of the changing trend of isoprene and monoterpene emissions as CAMS-GLOB-BIO v1.1 and CAMS-GLOB-BIO v3.1, e.g., three datasets all showed a strong increasing trend in Yunnan Province, and S1 shows much stronger changing trends comparing with the other three datasets with annually updated LAI and PFT datasets. The meteorological inputs for CAMS-GLOB-BIO v1.1 and v3.1 are ERA-Interim and ERA-5 reanalysis data, respectively, and the WRF model used in this study was also driven by ERA-Interim reanalysis data. Therefore, the four datasets have the same source of meteorological inputs. In addition, these estimations all adopted the same PFT-level emission factors from Guenther et al. (2012). Therefore, the potential reason for the differences of isoprene and monoterpene emission among the datasets in Figs. 10 and 11 is the discrepancies of PFT and LAI inputs. CAMS-GLOB-BIO also adopted the annually updated LAI inputs developed by Yuan et al. (2011) based on the MODIS MOD15A v5 LAI product, but the two versions of the CAMS-GLOB-BIO inventory did not show the same level of strong increasing trend as S1. The increasing trend of LAI in China is confirmed by multiple LAI products but with different rates (Piao et al., 2015; Chen et al., 2020). In this study, we adopted the latest MODIS LAI product of version 6, and a strong increasing trend of LAI in China has been found by using this product (Chen et al., 2019). Therefore, an increasing trend of BVOC emission induced by LAI should be seen in the estimation with annually updated LAI inputs, but the magnitude of this trend is also affected by the magnitude of changing trend of LAI products. The PFT map used in this study is coming from the MODIS land cover product, which is a mesoscale satellite product with the highest resolution of  $500\mathrm{m}$ . Besides the quality of the product, the method for converting the original land cover classification system to a PFT classification system is also important. Hartley et al. (2017) illustrated that the cross-walking table for converting land cover class maps to PFT fractional maps can lead to  $20\% -90\%$  uncertainties for gross primary production estimation in land surface models by using different vegetation fractions for mixed pixels, and the BVOC emission estimation has the same issue. In this study, we assumed that the pixels that were assigned as vegetation are  $100\%$  covered by that kind of vegetation (Table S1 in the Supplement). Therefore, this will lead to an overestimation of vegetation cover rate for mixed pixels, which can lead to higher BVOC emission.

references: Figure 5 compares modeled and satellite-retrieved tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns by county in China for the analyzed six cases. The first column in Fig. 5 compares the model and satellite data for all districts and counties in China for the summer of 2006. Modeled  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns are generally in good agreement with OMI  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns, with regression slopes varying from 0.78 to 1.01 and  $R^2$  values varying from 0.75 to 0.86. Simulations obtained using S1 substantially underestimate  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns compared to satellite-based columns, especially in densely populated regions. Tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns simulated using S2 present more pollution hotspots compared to S1, particularly in economically developed regions, such as the capital cities in each province. Using nighttime lights as a spatial proxy (S2)

To further understand the biases in the model simulations, we divide all counties into three categories – counties in four municipalities (i.e., Beijing, Tianjin, Shanghai, and Chongqing), urban counties, and suburban counties according to administrative district definition – and compare the modeled and observed  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns for these three categories (Fig. 5). For the four Chinese municipalities studied, emission totals are allocated from cities to counties; however, for other provinces, emissions are allocated from province to counties. Municipalities are defined as a separate category in the following analysis.
