references: for 2003 instead of 2005. Moreover, open burning of biomass was not included in Ohara et al. (2007). Regarding  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , Ohara et al. (2007) estimated a total emission of  $36.6\mathrm{Mt}$  for China in 2003, much higher than all other estimates for 2005 and later. This large discrepancy may come mainly from the difference between the updated domestic emission factors used in this study, and the foreign emission factor database that Ohara et al. (2007) relied more upon. Figure 5 compares the resulting emission inventory uncertainties with those of other studies, none of which included Monte Carlo simulation. The estimated uncertainty ranges for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  and  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  emissions are very similar among the available studies, while those of PM fractions of different sizes or carbonaceous species are significantly reduced. For example, the  $95\%$  CIs of  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions are estimated to be  $-17\sim 54\%$ ,  $-25\% \sim 136\%$ , and  $-40\% \sim 121\%$  in this study, respectively, much lower than  $-57\% \sim 130\%$ ,  $-68\% \sim 208\%$ , and  $-72\% \sim 258\%$  recently derived by Zhang et al. (2009d). The main reasons include: (1) the source categories in this study are more detailed, and random errors are thus significantly reduced due to the "compensation-of-error" mechanism (the same statistical mechanism described for the power sector comparison in Sect. 4.4); and (2) emission factors are

Abstract. The uncertainties of a national, bottom-up inventory of Chinese emissions of anthropogenic  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ , and particulate matter (PM) of different size classes and carbonaceous species are comprehensively quantified, for the first time, using Monte Carlo simulation. The inventory is structured by seven dominant sectors: coal-fired electric power, cement, iron and steel, other industry (boiler combustion), other industry (non-combustion processes), transportation, and residential. For each parameter related to emission factors or activity-level calculations, the uncertainties, represented as probability distributions, are either statistically fitted using results of domestic field tests or, when these are lacking, estimated based on foreign or other domestic data. The uncertainties (i.e.,  $95\%$  confidence intervals around the central estimates) of Chinese emissions of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ , total PM,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , black carbon (BC), and organic carbon (OC) in 2005 are estimated to be  $-14\% \sim 13\%$ ,  $-13\% \sim 37\%$ ,  $-11\% \sim 38\%$ ,  $-14\% \sim 45\%$ ,  $-17\% \sim 54\%$ ,  $-25\% \sim 136\%$ , and  $-40\% \sim 121\%$ , respectively. Variations at activity levels (e.g., energy consumption or industrial production) are not the main source of emission uncertainties. Due to narrow classification of source types, large sample sizes, and relatively high data quality, the coal-fired power sector is estimated to have the smallest emission uncertainties for all species except BC and OC. Due to poorer source classifications and a wider range of estimated emission factors, considerable uncertainties of  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  and PM emissions from cement production and boiler combustion in other industries are found. The probability distributions of emission factors for biomass burning, the largest source of BC and OC, are fitted based on very limited domestic field measurements, and special caution should thus be taken interpreting

Uncertainties introduced by energy statistics and applications of non-Chinese emission factors have been estimated in previous Chinese emission inventory studies. Streets et al. (2003) applied expert judgment, in which the coefficients of variation (i.e., standard deviation divided by the mean) of the activity levels were assumed based on measures of economic development and perceived statistical quality, and those of emission factors were based on the reliability ratings of U.S. emission factors (USEPA, 2002). They estimated that the uncertainties of Chinese emissions varied from  $-12\% \sim 13\%$  for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$  to  $-83\% \sim 495\%$  for organic carbon (expressed as the lower and upper bounds of a  $95\%$  confidence interval, CI, around a central estimate). To investigate discrepancies in the trends of bottom-up  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  emissions and of the  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  column by satellite, Zhang et al. (2007a) set alternative emission scenarios in which energy statistics from different sources and emission factors with different control levels were applied. They concluded that  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  emissions in east-central China in 2004 could range from 7.9 to  $9.3\mathrm{Tg}$ , and the annual growth rate of emissions from 1995 to 2004 could range from  $5.5\%$  to  $7.1\%$ . Other studies conducted preliminary uncertainty analysis of certain species or sectors in China using statistical methods. Bond et al. (2004) assumed that most emission factors of particulate matter (PM) followed lognormal distributions (with the exception of those of diesel vehicles, which had a gamma distribution) and evaluated the uncertainties of global black carbon (BC) and organic carbon (OC) emissions. The  $95\%$  CIs for Chinese emissions were estimated to be  $-37\% \sim 147\%$  and  $-44\% \sim 104\%$ , respectively. Wu et al. (2010) focused on

In this study, therefore, the uncertainties (expressed as the  $95\%$  CI around the central estimate) of a bottom-up emission inventory in China is evaluated with Monte Carlo simulations, combining comprehensive field measurements of domestic emission factors and investigations of activity levels. The species include  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ , total PM,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC. The target year is 2005 because it has the most available data. Determining the probability distributions of all of the related parameters is the main undertaking of this study. Section 2 reviews bottom-up emission inventory methods and analyzes the uncertainties of emission source fractions and corresponding activity levels. Section 3 is a thorough analysis of emission factors by sector and fuel type. Section 4 presents the results and related discussion of emission uncertainties by sector, sensitivity analysis, reliability and limitations of the analysis, and comparisons with other studies. Section 5 summarizes the present study.

Chinese emissions in 2005 with  $95\%$  CIs are shown in Table 1 by sector. To be consistent with previous studies, emissions from industrial boilers, cement plants, iron and steel plants, and other industrial processes are aggregated into a single category of industry emissions (see Table S8 in the Supplement for emissions and uncertainties in these sub-sectors). The uncertainties (expressed as the  $95\%$  CI around the central value) are estimated to be  $-14\% \sim 13\%$ ,  $-13\% \sim 37\%$ ,  $-11\% \sim 38\%$ ,  $-14\% \sim 45\%$ ,  $-17\% \sim 54\%$ ,  $-25\% \sim 136\%$ , and  $-40\% \sim 121\%$  for total emissions of  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ , PM,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC respectively (see Fig. S2 for the output distributions of those species by Monte Carlo simulation).

the result follow. The PM emission characteristics of residential coal combustion in China were tested by different researchers (Zhang and Smith, 2000; Chen et al., 2005; Zhang et al., 2008b), yielding varied emission factors. Very few estimates of the emission factors of Chinese combustion of biomass can be found, however, except for a series of measurements by Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b). Those measurements were made in four provinces with different types of biomass. As no other domestic tests are available for direct comparisons, systematic biases in these available measurements cannot be ruled out. In this study, we adopt the results from Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b) fully, lacking further domestic evidence. Given potential high uncertainties, however, a comparison test is conducted for OC emitted by its leading source, biomass combustion, in which the uncertainties of emission factors obtained from Li et al. (2007a, b, 2009b) are replaced with those of foreign studies (Andreae and Merlet, 2001; Streets et al., 2001; Bond et al., 2004). In this case the  $95\%$  CI of OC emission from biomass combustion changes from  $-58\sim 216\%$  to  $-31\%\sim 311\%$ , suggesting caution in interpretation of results based on limited domestic field measurements (see also the discussion in Sect. 4.3).

The situations for emission factors with higher reliability (i.e., A and B) are more complicated. For coal-fired power plants, the data on which the probability distributions for most parameters are fitted are obtained from both thorough field measurements by the authors and published studies by others (see details in Zhao et al., 2010). These support different emission factors across burner types, fuel qualities, and emission control levels. The uncertainty analysis for this sector are thus the most reliable in this study. Such confidence, however, does not apply to the residential sector, particularly biomass burning, even though the emission factors meet A or B criteria. As discussed in Sect. 4.1, the data on which the distributions are based are limited in number and systematic bias may result in this sector. In other words, the reliability of uncertainty analysis of biomass burning is in fact poor despite the statistical strength of data fitting. Given its high contributions to uncertainties in emission estimates, more field measurements of emissions from biomass burning in China are urgently needed.

Fig. 4. Comparisons of annual emissions for China from different studies. The emissions are for year 2005 unless noted. The range line represents the  $95\%$  CIs around the central estimates of this study.

Although inverse studies that evaluate emission inventories using observations and CTMs have indicated that Chinese  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  emissions may be underestimated by around  $50\%$  (Wang et al., 2004; Ma et al., 2006), the uncertainty estimated in this study is relatively small, ranging only  $-13\% \sim 37\%$ . The difference may result in part from omission in bottom-up inventories of anthropogenic  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  sources of entirely different character, such as microbial decomposition of organic wastes associated with the human-animal food chain and applications of chemical fertilizer (Wang et al., 2004). Moreover, emissions vary considerably across regions of China (Wang et al., 2007; Zhao and Wang, 2009) and seasons (Zhang et al., 2007a), and emission uncertainties differentiated accordingly may be larger than national estimates. In order to compare better emission estimates derived from satellite, aircraft, or surface observations, therefore, further investigation of the uncertainties of bottom-up inventories by regions and seasons is needed. These would require region-specific uncertainty assumptions for emission factors and activity levels, and seasonal distribution parameters.

metadata.title: Figure 1. Spatial patterns of (a) the total population density and (b) nighttime lights for eastern China and (c) the distributions of total population density and nighttime lights in China at a resolution of  0.1^{\circ} \times 0.1^{\circ} .
references: obtained from the LandScan population database (ORNL, 2006). The Gridded Population of the World (GPW) population map is also frequently used to allocate emissions, but we do not include it in our analysis because the uncertainties introduced by differences in population maps are minor (Andres et al., 2016). In the second dataset (S2), all emissions are allocated based on the nighttime lights map drawn from the Defense Meteorological Satellite Program Operational Linescan System (DMSP-OSL) satellite (http://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/dmsp/download_rad_cal_96-97.html). These two scenarios, which involve a single type of spatial proxy for all sectors, are simplifications of the common practice utilized in current emission inventories. We use S1 as the base case and make modifications upon it to evaluate different spatial proxies used in different sectors. Figure 1 compares the spatial distributions of the total population and nighttime light data. The nighttime light data present more significant urban-rural gradients than the total population density data and, thus, may better represent differences in economic development levels between urban and rural areas.

Abstract. Spatial proxies used in bottom-up emission inventories to derive the spatial distributions of emissions are usually empirical and involve additional levels of uncertainty. Although uncertainties in current emission inventories have been discussed extensively, uncertainties resulting from improper spatial proxies have rarely been evaluated. In this work, we investigate the impact of spatial proxies on the representation of gridded emissions by comparing six gridded  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission datasets over China developed from the same magnitude of emissions and different spatial proxies. GEOS-Chem-modeled tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns simulated from different gridded emission inventories are compared with satellite-based columns. The results show that differences between modeled and satellite-based  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns are sensitive to the spatial proxies used in the gridded emission inventories. The total population density is less suitable for allocating  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions than nighttime light data because population density tends to allocate more emissions to rural areas. Determining the exact locations of large emission sources could significantly strengthen the correlation between modeled and observed  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns. We then develop six sets of gridded  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission data using the same magnitude of emissions from the MEIC and different spatial proxies, as presented in Table 3. Many geographic information system (GIS) grid-based spatial proxies (e.g., population density and road networks) are at a resolution of  $1\mathrm{km}\times 1\mathrm{km}$ . In this work, emissions are first gridded at a resolution of  $1\mathrm{km}\times 1\mathrm{km}$  and then regridded to a resolution of  $0.667^{\circ}$  long  $\times 0.5^{\circ}$  lat to fit the GEOS-Chem model. The first two emission datasets evaluate two types of common spatial proxy maps: population distributions and nighttime lights. In the first gridded emission dataset (S1), all emissions are allocated based on population densities

Model underestimation in S1 mainly occurs for urban counties (Fig. 5c, slope = 0.53 and NMB = -20.3%). This approach, which uses the total population as a spatial proxy, assumes that the per capita emissions in different regions in a province are the same. However, because of varied industrial levels and economic patterns, per capita emissions can be very different across regions. According to Lamsal et al. (2013), the tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  column is a power law scaling function of the population size, and the exponent is affected by regional differences in per capita emissions. Using population density as a spatial proxy in these areas significantly underestimates the emissions in urban areas, as shown in Fig. 5c. Using nighttime lights to allocate emissions can significantly improve the model's performance for urban areas (Fig. 5g, slope = 1.02 and NMB = -1.8%), although overestimations are identified in a few counties. This finding demonstrates the feasibility of using nighttime lights alone as a spatial proxy when more complex indicators are not available. After determining the exact positions of power plant emissions, the model simulations are substantially improved for all types of regions (Fig. 5j-l); however, the urban emissions are still underestimated (slope = 0.69 and NMB =

The spatial proxies used when developing gridded emission inventories are empirical and can introduce uncertainties in bottom-up emission inventories. This issue has rarely been evaluated. In this work, we evaluate the effects of spatial proxies on the representations of spatial distributions of emissions using an integrated framework of bottom-up emission inventories, a chemical transport model, and satellite observations. We first develop six sets of gridded  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions for China using the same magnitude of emissions from the MEIC and different spatial proxies. The spatial proxies considered in this study include the following: total population, nighttime lights, locations of power plants, IGDP, vehicle populations, and two different road network datasets. The nested-grid GEOS-Chem model is then used to simulate tropospheric  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns using the six gridded emissions, and modeled  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns are compared to satellite-based  $\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns derived from OMI data.

metadata.title: Fig. 6. The base value of CO-emission as a function of relative engine load. The measurements of Agrawal, Moldanova and Sarvi have been shown, and the CO-base emission factor curve is based on Sarvi (2008a). The emissions of CO are also influenced by rapid changes of relative engine load.
references: Assuming perfect combustion conditions, the amount of emitted  $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  can be estimated in a straightforward manner from the amount of fuel burned. However, the CO emissions are substantially dependent on engine load. The data based on three experimental studies and the modeled dependency of the base emission factor of CO as a function of engine load has been presented in Fig. 6. The CO base emission factor as described by Sarvi (2008a) has been adopted in STEAM2,

Emissions of particulate matter and  $\mathrm{SO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  depend on the fuel consumption of the ship, whereas emissions of  $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$  mainly depend on the temperature and the duration of the combustion cycle. Emissions of carbon monoxide depend not only on engine load and engine power, but also on the gradient of engine power. Acceleration of ship results in incomplete combustion of fuel and relatively higher emissions of CO. As discussed previously, fuel consumption is dependent on engine load; the emissions of several pollutants have the same dependency. Several authors have reported experimental results on the composition of particulate matter as a function of engine load (Agrawal et al., 2008a, b, 2010; Petzold et al., 2008; Moldanova et al., 2009; Sarvi et al., 2008a) and sulphur content (Sarvi et al., 2008b; Buhaug et al., 2009). These datasets represent cases where measurements over the whole load range with several types of fuel with variable sulphur content were available.
metadata.concise_summary: The figure displays the base value of CO emissions as a function of relative engine load, comparing data from Agrawal, Moldanova, and Sarvi. It includes a CO-base emission factor curve.
metadata.inferred_insight: The figure illustrates how CO emissions decrease with increased engine load and highlights variability due to rapid load changes, reinforcing the importance of load in emission modeling.

metadata.title: Fig. 7. The predictions of the STEAM and STEAM2 models and the corresponding measured engine power. The data has been measured for a  60000t  RoPax vessel that was sailing in the Baltic Sea within and near the archipelago surrounding the city of Stockholm in April 2008.
references: An example comparison between the predictions on main engine power of the two model versions is presented in Fig. 7. The engine power data has been collected in this study at the engine room of a large RoPax (Roll On - Roll Off cargo/Passenger) vessel using its own data logging systems. The presented voyage was done in an archipelago area near Stockholm, Sweden, and in the vicinity of this archipelago, in April 2008. We have used this specific dataset, as it was the only one available in the Baltic sea region. Measured power profiles, such as the one presented in Fig. 7, are difficult to obtain, as only a limited number of vessels have internal equipment suitable to collect this data.

The STEAM2 model determines the number of engines, which need to be operated to overcome the predicted resistance of the ship, and the engine load of all running engines. We have evaluated the performance of this sub-module, by using the data from the cruise presented above (cf. Fig. 7).
metadata.concise_summary: Figure 7 compares the predictions of the STEAM and STEAM2 models with the measured engine power of a RoPax vessel sailing near Stockholm in April 2008.
metadata.inferred_insight: The figure highlights the predictive accuracy of the STEAM and STEAM2 models against actual engine power data, emphasizing the model's performance in operational conditions.

references: Table 1 and Fig. 11 show the top-down estimates for Chinese annual NMVOC emissions from the four inversion experiments and compare them against the a priori. Our annual total top-down estimates for Chinese NMVOCs ranged from 30.7 to  $49.5\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$ , compared to the  $38.3\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  of the a priori. The highest top-down estimate was from IE-3, constrained by 1.7 times the GOME-2A formaldehyde VCD observations. The lowest top-down estimate was from IE-2, mainly driven by the relatively low formaldehyde observations from OMI.

Anthropogenic sources constituted  $44\% - 53\%$  of the total top-down NMVOC emissions. The lowest total top-down anthropogenic emission estimate was from IE-2  $(16.4\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1})$ . All four inversion experiments consistently showed larger annual emissions of anthropogenic glyoxal precursors than the a priori (Fig. 11). In particular, our top-down estimates for anthropogenic aromatics ranged from 5.5 to  $7.9\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$ , consistently larger than the a priori of  $5.4\mathrm{Tgyr}^{-1}$  (Li et al., 2017). metadata.save_path: C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Exhaust Gas Condensation during Engine Cold Start\figure_5.jpg
metadata.title: Figure 5. Vehicle Gasoline Direct Injection (G-DI). Left panels: First  600s  of WLTC. Right panels: NEDC (New European Driving Cycle). Upper:  H_2O  concentrations. Middle: Exhaust gas temperatures. Low: Speed profiles. Dashed boxes indicate the time period where the  H_2O  concentrations reached similar levels. The dashed line is the dew point of gasoline exhaust gas  53^{\circ}C
references: To further understand the differences, different test cycles were conducted (WLTC or NEDC) with different engine conditions: cold start, hot start (immediately after the cold start test without switching off the engine), warm start (30 min after a cycle). Figure 5 summarizes the results. The  $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$  measurements based on FTIR are different at the beginning of the cycle. They reach similar levels after  $250~s$  for the WLTC and after  $850~s$  for the NEDC. These times correspond to a temperature of the exhaust gas at the sampling location of around  $56^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which is just above the dew point of the gasoline exhaust gas  $(53^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ .

Figure 5. Vehicle Gasoline Direct Injection (G-DI). Left panels: First  $600\mathrm{s}$  of WLTC. Right panels: NEDC (New European Driving Cycle). Upper:  $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$  concentrations. Middle: Exhaust gas temperatures. Low: Speed profiles. Dashed boxes indicate the time period where the  $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$  concentrations reached similar levels. The dashed line is the dew point of gasoline exhaust gas  $(53^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$

We believe that the differences during the first seconds of a test are due to condensation of water on the cold surfaces of the vehicle exhaust pipes (assuming that the instruments sampling lines are heated). When the surfaces reach the dew point temperature of the exhaust gas, the differences become negligible (as speculated in Figure 5). The water condensation at the tailpipe has been observed by some researchers [17,20]. The low  $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$  concentration at the beginning of a cold start test is something that has been seen [19], but no special attention has been given as until recently measurements from the tailpipe were not required by the legislation.

It should be emphasized that the differences have to do with condensation taking place at the tailpipe and aftreatment devices and not with the engine temperature. A warm engine start with tailpipe surface temperatures below the dew point also showed similar (but smaller) effects (Figure 5). On the other hand, a hot engine start with tailpipe surface temperatures above the dew point showed no condensation (Figure 5).

metadata.inferred_insight: The figure demonstrates that H2O concentrations stabilize at similar levels when exhaust temperature reaches the dew point, emphasizing the impact of condensation on tailpipe measurements in different test cycles.

metadata.save_path: C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Comparison of measured and model-calculated real-w\figure_5.jpg
metadata.title: Fig. 5. Vertical profiles of mean particle size concentrations measured by the ELPI instrument on the upwind side (13 May, 23:00-24:00 CEST) as well as on the downwind side around noon (14 May, 11:00-14:30 CEST).
references: For the first time, vertical profiles with high temporal and spatial resolution of particulate air pollutants could be recorded by tower elevators (Imhof et al., 2005). Upwind the motorway, no height-dependent difference in the particle number and surface area concentration was observed. On the downwind side, substantially higher concentrations were measured within the plume from near the ground up to  $20\mathrm{m}$  height than above the plume (Fig. 5). This height dependency was found for particles with diameter  $< 300\mathrm{nm}$ , which are soot particles and nucleation mode particles formed by condensation of exhaust gases in most cases and for coarse particles (diameter  $>1\mu \mathrm{m}$ ), originating from tyre and brake abrasion and resuspension of soil dust. Between 300 and  $700\mathrm{nm}$  particle size, no height dependence was found, because motor vehicles emit only a few particles in this size range.

After adjusting the instruments for their different time lags, the vertical distribution of the particle number concentrations showed a similar behaviour as the  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  concentrations: No height dependence on the upwind side and a continuous decrease with increasing height from 5 to  $30\mathrm{m}$  above the earth's surface on the downwind side was found. Consequently, the results in this study confirmed the correlation between particles and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  values that had also been observed in former case studies. A linear relation between the particle surface area (SA) and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  with correlation coefficients of  $R^2 = 0.96$  and 0.89 for 2 downwind cases was found. At around  $30\mathrm{m}$  above ground, the background value of SA was attained. By subtracting the background value from the concentrations measured in the near-surface air layer, direct emissions of the road were obtained, even if contemporaneous upwind and downwind measurements were missing. Together with the parallel wind measurements, mean absolute emission factors (EF) were determined. The PM emissions in the range between  $30\mathrm{nm}$  and  $10\mu \mathrm{m}$  from all vehicles passing the site were found to be  $1.80\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\mathrm{km}^{-1}\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ . Separation into the categories of LDV led to  $1.22\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\mathrm{km}^{-1}\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.03\mathrm{cm}^3\mathrm{km}^{-1}\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  aerosol volume, while the values of HDV were  $7.79\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\mathrm{km}^{-1}\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.41\mathrm{cm}^3\mathrm{km}^{-1}$  veh $^{-1}$  particle volume.

Fig. 4. Vertical profiles of mean particle size distributions measured by SMPS instruments at three different heights on the upwind side of the motorway (13 May 2001, midnight) and on the downwind side (14 May 2001, morning rush hour and noon).

metadata.source_md: Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga.md
metadata.save_path: C:\liuxingyu\multisubspace-data\predataprocess\paper_md\Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga\figure_7.jpg
metadata.title: Fig. 7. Change in ambient concentrations of OVOCs contributed by different sources during the dry seasons of 2014-2019.
references: Fig. 7 presents the changes in absolute concentrations of OVOCs contributed by different sources during the dry seasons for the six-year study period. To better depict the contribution trends of different OVOC sources, the parameters were normalized (based on

Oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) are important precursors and intermediate products of atmospheric photochemical reactions, which can promote the formation of secondary pollutants such as ozone  $(\mathrm{O}_3)$  and secondary organic aerosol (SOA). However, there have been few studies on the sources of and long-term variation in ambient OVOCs. This study combined sensitive, near real-time measurements of VOCs by proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) with an improved photochemical age parameterization method to quantify daytime sources of OVOCs in an urban atmosphere in China from 2014 to 2019, permitting the observation of the impacts of emission control strategies that were implemented during this period. Temporal variation in six key OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), formic acid, and acetic acid) were observed. The sum of concentrations of OVOCs was averagely  $13\%$  higher during the dry season (November to April), when winds transported polluted air masses to Shenzhen from the continent, than during the wet season, and peak diurnal levels occurred during the daytime year-round due to photochemical production and higher daytime anthropogenic emissions. The average dry season concentration of OVOCs declined from a peak of 30.3 ppb in 2015 to 18.7 ppb in 2019. The results of source apportionment showed that primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to methanol, MEK, and acetic acid  $(32 - 51\%)$ ; the dominant sources of acetaldehyde and formic acid were both primary and secondary anthropogenic sources; and biomass burning contributed a small fraction  $(5 - 11\%)$  to the six OVOCs. From 2014 to 2019, contributions from primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs decreased significantly by  $50 - 60\%$  due to intensive pollution control measures in Shenzhen, whereas pollution control measures had no observable impact on secondary OVOCs, indicating their formation was not limited by availability of their primary VOC precursors.

Previous studies have proven that some low-carbon OVOCs (formaldehyde and acetaldehyde) can generate OH radicals and hydroperoxyl radicals  $\mathrm{(HO_2)}$  through photolysis to affect the oxidizing capacity of the atmosphere (Volkamer et al., 2010; Shao et al., 2011; Xue et al., 2016). OVOCs can also participate in photochemical reactions and contribute to the generation of  $\mathrm{O_3}$  and SOA (Louie et al., 2013; Ehn et al., 2014). In addition, as most OVOCs are highly water soluble, they can be absorbed into the human body, irritating the eyes, skin and respiratory tract, thus harming human health. OVOCs such as formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, acetone and acrolein have been shown to pose serious health risks (WHO, 2000, Delfino et al., 2003; Bashkin, 2009; Du et al., 2014). Given the important role of OVOCs in photochemical reactions and the complexity of their sources, OVOCs have been the subject of extensive research recently (Sahu et al., 2017; Maji et al., 2020), especially in China, where air pollution is severe (Zhang et al., 2012, 2015; Zhou et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). Zhang et al. (2012) studied the pollution characteristics of BTEX (benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, m,p-xylene and o-xylene) and carbonyl compounds during summer in Beijing and found that the  $\mathrm{O_3}$  formation potential of carbonyl compounds  $(166.1~\mu \mathrm{g} / \mathrm{m}^3)$  was approximately 2.5 times that of BTEX  $(65.4~\mu \mathrm{g} / \mathrm{m}^3)$ . Zhou et al. (2019) used proton transfer reaction mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) to measure VOCs in Xi'an and used the positive matrix factorization (PMF) model to analyze over 30 types of VOCs and identify eight primary sources and found that local emissions, such as industrial processes, solvent usage, residential biofuel use and vehicular exhaust, contributed about  $70\%$  of the VOCs measured. Zhu et al. (2019) used PTR-MS to monitor OVOCs during different seasons and applied photochemical age parameter analysis to evaluate the sources of OVOCs in Shenzhen. They found that primary anthropogenic sources were the most important source of OVOCs in urban areas of China.

Shenzhen belongs to the subtropical marine monsoon climate zone. Marked differences in the four seasons were not evident, and the observation time each year was slightly different; therefore, this study divided the entire year into a dry season and a wet season according to the monthly temperature and precipitation. As presented in Figure S1, the period from May to October, with higher temperatures and abundant precipitation, was defined as the wet season, and the period from November to April, with less precipitation, was defined as the dry season. The meteorological data are from the Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality (Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). In addition to the differences in temperature and precipitation, the sources of air masses in Shenzhen during the wet and dry seasons were also very different. Taking January and July 2018 as an example, the 48-h backward trajectories at  $100\mathrm{m}$  above ground level in Shenzhen  $(113.9^{\circ}\mathrm{E}, 22.6^{\circ}\mathrm{N})$  were calculated beginning at 12:00 UTC using the Hybrid Single Particle Lagrangian Integrated Trajectory (HYSPLIT) model (Draxler and Rolph, 2003). The results show that the origin of the air mass passing through Shenzhen in different seasons was very different: during the dry season, the air mass mainly came from the northern mainland, and during the wet season, it mainly came from the South China Sea, which has also been explained in detail in our previous studies (Zou et al., 2017). In addition, referencing existing studies of air pollution in the Pearl River Delta, many scholars have adopted a method of dividing the wet and dry seasons (Cui et al., 2015). Figure S2 shows the changes in major meteorological parameters during the six-year observation period in Shenzhen. Analysis of major meteorological parameters in Shenzhen (Fig S2) did not indicate significant interannual variability in temperature, rainfall, humidity or wind conditions that could have substantially influenced the long-term variation in atmospheric pollutants observed in this study.

PRD and to improve the accuracy of the source apportionment. First, we chose benzene as the tracer of primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs. Generally, CO (de Gouw et al., 2005, 2018), acetylene (Yuan et al., 2012) and benzene (Ma et al., 2016; Zhu et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019) are commonly used tracers representing anthropogenic sources of VOCs. Anthropogenic emissions of VOCs in China are rather complicated, with industrial solvents and motor vehicle exhaust contributing significantly to VOCs. However, CO and acetylene mainly come from combustion emissions, which cannot represent well the anthropogenic emissions of VOCs in China. Compared with CO and acetylene, benzene can better reflect the anthropogenic emissions of VOCs in the PRD. For example, Zhang et al. (2013) used the PMF model to analyze the source of VOCs in the PRD and found that combustion processes and industrial emissions were the predominant sources of benzene. Second, considering the photolysis of aldehydes and ketones during the daytime, which obviously conflicts with assumption (2), we need to consider the effect of the photodegradation of aldehydes and ketones on their removal. Referring to the studies of Wang et al. (2017) and Huang et al. (2020), we introduced a coefficient  $(\mathrm{m})$  before the OH reaction rate constant  $\left(\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{ovoc}}\right)$  to compensate for the daytime loss of aldehydes and ketones caused by photolysis. A detailed description of the calculation method of the m value is given in the supporting information. For methanol, formic acid and acetic acid, with almost no photolysis (Saunders et al., 2003), this study ignored the effect of photolysis on daytime removal (set m as 1), while the calculated m values for acetaldehyde, acetone and methyl ethyl ketone (MEK) were 1.02, 1.26 and 1.25, respectively, during the sampling period. Third, previous studies have shown that biomass burning emissions are an inevitable source of OVOCs (Yuan et al., 2010; Warneke et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2016). For example, Yuan et al. (2010) reported that biomass burning emissions in the PRD contributed  $9.5 - 17.7\%$  to nine kinds of VOCs (including methanol, acetone, and MEK), suggesting that the contribution of biomass burning to OVOCs cannot be ignored. Therefore, the last parameter  $(ER_{bb} * [\text{Tracer}_{bb}])$  in Equation (1) was added in this study to estimate the contribution of biomass burning to OVOCs.  $[\text{Tracer}_{bb}]$  represents the concentration of ambient acetonitrile, which is an excellent indicator for biomass burning and can be accurately measured by PTR-MS (de Gouw et al., 2003b). In addition, previous studies have shown that benzene mainly comes from transportation and solvent use in the PRD, and biomass burning accounts for less than  $10\%$  (Lyu et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2013), so the influence of using benzene as a tracer of anthropogenic sources on biomass burning emissions can be ignored.  $\mathrm{ER}_{bb}$  is the emission factor of OVOCs relative to acetonitrile, which was calculated by least-squares fit along with  $\mathrm{ER}_{\mathrm{OVOC}}$ ,  $\mathrm{ER}_{\mathrm{precursor}}$ ,  $\mathrm{ER}_{\mathrm{biogen}}$ ,  $\mathrm{k}_{\mathrm{precursor}}$ , and [background].

It should be emphasized that because the sampling data of this study span six years, in addition to the above assumptions, one more assumption should be added: (4) during the sampling period, there was no significant change in the ratio of OVOC anthropogenic emissions to tracers (benzene) in Shenzhen. The emission reduction strategy of Shenzhen is to reduce emission sources of pollutants as a whole, such as replacing gasoline vehicles with electric vehicles and closing down or moving VOC-emitting enterprises to other areas. These measures have reduced emissions of VOCs, but the emission ratio among specific VOC species is relatively stable. Toluene/benzene (T/B) was considered a good indicator to judge the contribution of motor vehicle sources and solvent use to VOC emissions (Nelson and Quigley, 1984; Wang et al., 2002; Araizaga et al., 2013). Figure S5 shows the changes in the concentrations of benzene and toluene and T/B from 2014 to 2019. A total of 17 species of VOCs were monitored in this study by PTR-MS from 2014 to 2019, including 6 species classified as OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, formic acid, acetic acid, and MEK); 5 aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene, styrene, C8 and C9 aromatics); 3 classified as biogenic species (isoprene, MVK + MACR, and monoterpenes); and 2 species that, in addition to benzene, were measured as tracers (acetonitrile and DMS). The compounds, the respective  $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{z}$  of their parent ion signal, and their detection limits are provided in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 2, the average of the sum of the VOC concentrations in the dry season over the six years ranged from 25.3 to 41.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 33.1 ppbv; for the wet season, the average value ranged from 23.0 to 39.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 29.4 ppbv. In terms of the proportion of each component, in the six years of dry and wet seasons, OVOCs (70–78%) accounted for the largest proportion, followed by aromatic hydrocarbons (16–26%), whereas the concentrations of biogenic VOCs (BVOCs) and tracers were relatively low (less than 10%). Figure S6 shows the time series of 17 species of VOCs during the sampling period. To evaluate the concentrations of OVOCs in this study, comparative results of the concentrations of six OVOCs in five urban areas in China are listed in Table 1. Compared to Guangzhou and Hong Kong, two other mega-cities in the PRD region, the concentrations of acetaldehyde, acetone, and MEK in Shenzhen were higher than those in Hong

The parameterization method based on photochemical aging described in section 2.4 was used to analyze the sources of OVOCs during the daytime (8:00-20:00). Referencing the source analysis results for the OVOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019 (in Table 2), the measured concentrations of six OVOC species monitored by PTR-MS were in good agreement with the simulated concentrations, and the measured values of each OVOC species were highly correlated with the simulated values (R was between 0.71 and 0.86, except for formic acid, which was 0.62). For the six-year average, the sources of methanol and MEK were similar, with the largest contribution from primary anthropogenic sources  $(47 - 51\%)$  followed by biogenic and background sources. The contributions to acetaldehyde and acetic acid from primary and secondary anthropogenic sources were similar  $(26 - 32\%)$  followed by biogenic sources (approximately  $20\%$ ). In contrast to other OVOCs, acetone was the most affected by regional background concentration  $(37\%)$  which may be because acetone is relatively inactive and widely exists in the atmosphere, and its ground-level background mixing ratios can reach 500 pptv throughout the troposphere under extremely clean conditions (Seinfeld and Pandis, 2016). For formic acid, secondary anthropogenic sources contributed the most  $(34\%)$

Fig. 6 shows the trends in the relative contributions to OVOCs from 2014 to 2019 during the dry and wet seasons. In general, during the six years from 2014 to 2019, primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to OVOCs during Shenzhen's dry season, but the proportion exhibited a decreasing trend year over year, and the proportion during the six-year period decreased by approximately  $15\%$  overall. This continuous decreasing trend indicates that the VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas, where most of the industry and traffic of Shenzhen is located, had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. The OVOC source compositions during the wet season, when clean marine air masses from the south dominated the sampling site, did not exhibit a clear year-by-year trend. The contributions of secondary anthropogenic sources to OVOC concentrations (except that of methanol) during the wet season were significantly higher (approximately 1.3-1.6 times greater) than those during the dry season, indicating that secondary transformation of NMHCs during the wet season contributed greatly to OVOC concentrations. Biogenic sources reasonably contributed a larger proportion of OVOC concentrations during the wet season, whereas biomass burning contributed a larger fraction of acetaldehyde during the dry season.

Among the six OVOC species, the species with the largest decrease in concentration attributed to primary anthropogenic sources was methanol, which decreased by 7 ppb. However, contributions of the other sources to the absolute concentrations of OVOCs did not significantly change over the six-year period. Specifically, annual variations in secondary anthropogenic sources were not significant, indicating that secondary formation of atmospheric OVOCs in this region might be restricted by the atmospheric oxidizing capacity and that precursor emissions are more than abundant.

VOC emission reduction in industrial enterprises and the coating industry occurs mainly through upgrading emission standards and closing down industries with high VOC emissions. For example, furniture manufacturing used to be an important industry in Shenzhen but also a key VOC-emitting industry. From 2010 to 2016, 565 coating production lines were removed or relocated in Shenzhen, reducing VOC emissions by more than 30,000 tons every year. Since 2017, Shenzhen has completely banned solvent paints and adhesives in the manufacturing process and replaced them with water-based paints to further reduce VOC emissions, especially the emissions of aldehydes and ketones commonly used in the production of furniture manufacturing (Ecological Environment Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). As a commonly used tracer of anthropogenic sources of VOCs, the annual average concentration of benzene during the dry season from 2014 to 2019 was 0.98, 0.81, 0.64, 0.61, 0.49, and 0.45 ppb (Figure S5), respectively, reflecting the decreasing trend in primary VOC emissions in recent years. Therefore, the decrease in OVOC concentrations during the dry season over the six-year study period could primarily be attributed to decreases in the contributions from primary anthropogenic sources. PTR-MS was used for continuous online monitoring of atmospheric VOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019. The results revealed that OVOCs were the most abundant of 17 species of VOCs measured, comprising  $70 - 78\%$ . The six-year mean concentration for the dry season was  $33.1~\mathrm{ppb}$ , approximately  $13\%$  higher than that for the wet season of  $22.9~\mathrm{ppb}$ . There were diurnal variations in OVOC concentrations, with higher concentrations during the daytime. The diurnal variation in acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\mathrm{O}_3$ , and it had the largest OFP of the OVOC species, indicating that acetaldehyde was an important intermediate of photochemical reactions. The results from source apportionment based on photochemical aging parameterization revealed that contributions of OVOCs from primary anthropogenic sources exhibited an evident declining trend of  $50 - 60\%$  from 2014 to 2019 during the dry season, indicating that the intensive VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas of the sampling site had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. However, to reduce the impact of OVOCs on air quality in Shenzhen, more efforts are needed to control OVOCs from anthropogenic secondary sources by reducing not only their primary VOC precursors but also their complex atmospheric oxidizing capacity, which will finally restrict the production of  $\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA.

metadata.title: Fig. 9. Comparison of the ratios of measured and model-calculated source strengths of 21 NMHC species measured during BAB II.
references: Excluding the data of 8 May, 20-22 Central European Summer Time (CEST), a period with poorly fulfilled prerequisites for source strength calculations, the difference between measurements and calculations is reduced to  $18\%$ . This finding underlines the relatively good emission factors for benzene from roller test stand investigations compared to most of the other NMHC compounds. The comparison of all 21 ratios of measured and model-calculated source strengths is shown in Fig. 9. In general, the measured values are higher than the modelled ones. Overall 17 measured NMHC compounds are in a range of 1.3-7 times higher than modelled, whereas 4 compounds are between 11 and 21 times higher than the modelled ones. The measured value for i-butane, benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene and 1,2,4-trimethylbenzene/decane are within the standard deviation in a good agreement with the modelled values. For the other compounds the difference between model results and measurements is higher than the standard deviation.

The quality of an emission calculation model based on emission factors measured on roller test stands and statistical traffic data was evaluated using source strengths and emission factors calculated from real-world exhaust gas concentration differences measured upwind and downwind of a motorway in southwest Germany. Gaseous and particulate emissions were taken into account. Detailed traffic census data were taken during the measurements. The results were compared with findings of similar studies.

The source strengths of 21 non-methane hydrocarbon (NMHC) compounds quantified are underestimated by the model. The ratio between the measured and model-calculated emissions ranges from 1.3 to 2.1 for BTX and up to 21 for 16 other NMHCs. The reason for the differences is the insufficient knowledge of NMHC emissions of road traffic.

The scope of this final paper within the special issue on the results of the BAB II project is (i) to summarise the main results, (ii) to compare the measured and model-calculated source strengths of emissions, released by traffic on the motorway section under investigation and finally to evaluate the quality of the emission calculation model updated by the results of the traffic census made simultaneously. Although emission factors for a "mean vehicle" depending on the fleet composition passing the measurement site are given, the main objective is the improvement of the emission model by reliable traffic data and not by updated emission factors. These can not be estimated by the method used in BAB II, because the model needs emission factors of individual vehicle types (motors) as input data rather than emission factors valid for a fleet of vehicles passing the site randomly distributed. The remaining differences between measured and model-calculated source strengths is therefore mainly attributed to imprecise or even missing emission factors for gases, NMHC compounds and particulates from roller test stands.

As this paper summarises the BAB II results and compares measured and model calculated emissions, for detailed information about the determination of source strengths, traffic census data and model development look up the specific contributions within this special issue. For the overall project description see Corsmeier et al. (2005). The source strengths for CO and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  are given by Kohler et al. (2005), those for NMHC compounds by Petrea et al. (2005). PM emissions are investigated by Rosenbohm et al. (2005) and Imhof et al. (2005) and finally the emission model calculations and the traffic measurements are presented by Kuhlwein and Friedrich (2005).

Fig. 2. Measured and model-calculated weekly cycle of mean source strengths of CO and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  between the morning and evening rush hours, averaged over the time period of the BAB II campaign. The error bars indicate the standard error.

During BAB II, more than 50 NMHCs were detected and the source strengths of 21 of them were quantified (Petrea et al., 2005). Horizontal distribution of C4-C9 aliphatic and aromatic hydrocarbons on both sides of the motorway was monitored using a quasi on-line gas chromatograph (GC). Vertical NMHC concentration profiles were determined in 3 heights upwind and in 5 heights downwind of the motorway by means of adsorption tubes that were analysed by off-line GC. Good agreement between the quasi on-line and the offline measurements was found for 10 compounds measured with both systems. Generally, the relative uncertainty for these NMHCs ranged between  $10\%$  and  $30\%$  depending on the concentration level (Vogt et al., 2005).
Fig. 8. Comparison of measured and model-calculated source strengths of benzene for 12 BAB II periods.

During the BAB II campaign, over 50 NMHCs were detected (Petrea et al., 2005). The source strengths of 21 NMHC compounds could be calculated and were compared with model calculations by Kuhlwein and Friedrich (2005). For benzene the comparison of measured and model-calculated source strengths are given in Fig. 8 for 12 time intervals between 2 and  $4\mathrm{h}$  duration. There are cases of over- and underestimated model-calculated source strength compared to the measurements. On average the measurements are  $26\%$  higher than the model calculations for highest stage 3.

The calculations of source strengths and the evaluation of emission factors were carried out for major components of traffic emissions, i.e. CO,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and 21 NMHC compounds. An important outcome of BAB II is that, in general, the measured emissions of both gaseous compounds CO and  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  are larger than the model-calculated emissions by  $23\%$  and  $17\%$ , respectively. Using multiple regression, it can be shown that the major discrepancy between  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  simulations and measurements is caused by HDV. This indicates that the emission factors given for  $\mathrm{NO}_x$  are still too low, especially for HDV  $(22\%)$ . For CO, the HDV and LDV fractions could not be separated, but it may be assumed that the CO emission factor is influenced mainly by LDV and that the given emission factor also is too low  $(23\%)$ .

metadata.title: Fig. 1. Photos showing the (a) coating process and (b) sampling equipment.
references: The description of the source samples is tabulated in Table 1. Three coating sectors in China were investigated in this study, namely cargo container coating, ship coating, and furniture coating. The coating processes were divided into primary coating, secondary coating, and tertiary coating, depending on the manufacturing flows in each industrial sector. In general, there was one worker in each coating workshop under normal working conditions. The workshops were  $60 - 150\mathrm{m}^2$  for different coating processes in these industrial sectors. The central ventilation system was installed in the workplaces and the exhaust gas was discharged from the chimney and stack. The activated carbon-based masks were used by the workers. Whole-air samples were collected in the coating workshops that were being operated under normal conditions. Fig. 1(a) shows an example of the working condition of furniture coating.

Indoor air was collected using 3.2-L fused silica stainless steel canisters that had been precleaned with high-purity nitrogen and evacuated with an automated canister cleaner. Each sample was taken for  $30\mathrm{min}$  using an inlet with pre-calibrated flow regulator (CS1200 sampler; Entech Instrument, Inc.) operating at  $38~\mathrm{mL~min}^{-1}$  (Entech Instruments Inc., California, USA), as shown in Fig. 1(b). The sampling equipment was held by the sampling staff at about  $1.5\mathrm{-m}$  high, next to the occupational painter and less than  $1\mathrm{m}$  away from the spraying source to represent a similar distance with the painter in real situations. Three samples were collected at similar position in each workshop to ensure the representative of the field samples. Canister vacuums were checked before and after sampling. The field samples and two blank samples were sent to the Peking University (PKU) VOCs laboratory for analysis immediately after sampling within one week of shipping time. The blank

Solvent use and paint consumption are significant source sectors of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) emissions in China. The occupational painters have high risk of health effect due to exposure to high VOCs concentration. However, the toxic components in coating environment have not been carefully identified, and the health risks of VOCs exposure have not been sufficiently assessed. This study collected air samples from nine workshops of three major coating sectors in the Yangtze River Delta of China, namely cargo container coating, ship equipment coating, and furniture coating, to evaluate the noncancer and cancer risk of toxic VOCs exposure to occupational painters under a normal working condition. The results show that the container coating had highest cancer risk  $(2.29 \times 10^{-6} - 5.53 \times 10^{-6})$  exceeding the safe limit of  $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ , while non-cancer risk of all workshops was lower than acceptable level of 1. Ethylbenzene and 1,2-dichloropropane should be targeted for priority removal during the container coating process in attempt to reduce adverse health effect on the occupational painters. This study helps better understand the health risk of VOCs exposure in coating workshops in China and provides information for policy-makers to formulate possible control of specific toxic compounds during coating process.

metadata.title: Fig. 1. Flow chart of coke production and VOCs emission sampling sites.
references: A flow chart of coke production and the VOC sampling sites (A–D) are shown in Fig. 1. The sites included stack flue gas from the non-recovery coking plant (SFN; Site A) and

The source profiles, emission factors, and chemical reactivity of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) generated by two typical coking plants, one employing a non-recovery process and the other, a mechanical process, in Shanxi Province, a major coking hub in China, were determined for different segments of coke production during normal operations. The primary components in the stack flue gas were ethylene, 1-butene, benzene, acetylene, and 2,2-dimethylbutane for the non-recovery plant and styrene, benzene, and ethylene for the mechanical plant. The fugitive emissions were also monitored at the mechanical plant, and the most abundant species leaking from the oven were benzene, toluene, ethane,  $m$ -xylene, and ethylene, whereas those leaking from byproducts were benzene, propane, ethane, ethylene,  $n$ -pentane,  $n$ -butane, isobutene, 1-butene, toluene, and propylene. The stack flue gas at the non-recovery and mechanical coking plants exhibited VOC emission factors of  $96 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke and  $0.4 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke, respectively; thus, VOCs released by the former merit greater concern. Since the highest ozone formation potential (OFP) was observed for the stack flue gas at the non-recovery plant ( $80.26 \mathrm{mg} \mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), followed by fugitive oven emissions at the mechanical plant ( $7.22 \mathrm{mg} \mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), controlling these VOCs will significantly reduce their conversion into ozone. Overall, replacing non-recovery coking plants with mechanical coking plants will decrease VOC emissions and improve the ambient air quality in China.

implemented from 2008–2014, it still accounted for 8 million tons of coke in 2017 (CCIA, 2019). Mechanical coking plants produce coke in a carbonization chamber, then recover the byproducts from the coke oven gas, including tar, benzene, and ammonia. Differently, in non-recovery coking plants, the byproducts are burned and the heat is recovered during the coal conversion process (He, 2006). The emission pathways of pollutants vary with different coking process. Pollutants can be discharged into atmosphere from stack, coke oven leaks, or the byproduct production area in a mechanical coking plant. However, for non-recovery coking plants, pollutants are predominantly emitted from the stack; coke oven leaks can be neglected under the negative pressure conditions of the coke oven. Previous studies have found that pollutants such as particulate matter, nitrogen oxide, sulfur dioxide, toxic metals, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are released into the environment during coke production (Mu et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2014; Mu et al., 2014). VOCs emitted from the coking process account for the largest proportion (12.8%) of all VOCs emitted from industrial processes (Wu et al., 2017). Long-term exposure to these pollutants, especially PAHs and VOCs, can lead to adverse health effects in coke plant workers (He et al., 2015).

The source profiles and emission factors of VOCs are key information for establishing VOCs emission inventories, source apportionment, environmental management, etc. VOCs source profiles and emission factors have been widely researched in the USA, and the results have been integrated into the SPECIATE 4.5 database (U.S. EPA, 2016) and EPA AP-42 (U.S. EPA, 2008), respectively. The emission factors of various sources have also been identified in other countries, such as Denmark (EMEP, 2016) and UK (NAEI, 2018). Considering the large uncertainty inherent in referencing the results from other countries, scientists in China have recently obtained some VOCs' domestic source profiles and emission factors (Shi et al., 2015; Yan et al., 2016; Zhong et al., 2017; Shen et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018; Zhong et al., 2018). For example, for the coking industry, He (2006) reported that C4 and C6 hydrocarbons are the most abundant VOCs in the coking oven stack flue gas from a coke plant, whereas C6 and C8 hydrocarbons are dominant in coal charging and coke discharging. By comprehensively evaluating the coking process and VOCs from the top of the coke oven, Li (2009) revealed that the major VOCs emissions from coke production are benzene, ethane, ethylene, propylene, toluene, propane, and 1-butene. Jia et al. (2009) identified the major VOCs species of coke oven gas as ethylene, ethane, propene, propane, benzene, toluene, and xylene, and showed that the concentration of VOCs from the coal charging process is significantly higher than that from coking. Moreover, Shi et al. (2015) showed that toluene and  $n$ -hexane are the most abundant species in coke production, whereas Li et al. (2018) found that the five most abundant species emitted from the coking process are acetylene, benzene, ethane, toluene, and propene, accounting for  $>86\%$  of total VOCs. The source profiles of VOCs for the coking process differ between different studies. This suggests that VOCs from different emission segments or processes may affect the overall source profile for coke production.

In terms of VOCs emission factors from Chinese coke production, He (2006) calculated a VOCs emission factor via stack flue gas of  $487.16\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke. Emission factors of VOCs from coal charging  $(56.84\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke) and coke discharging  $(1.26\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke) have also been reported by Li (2009). In the emission factor guidebooks of U.S. EPA (2008), NAEI (2018), and EMEP (2016), the emission factors of VOCs were given as  $68\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke,  $41\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke, and  $47\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  coal, respectively. This difference between VOC emission factors from different studies could lead to uncertainty in emission estimates. Moreover, previous source profiles only consider the coking process of mechanical coking plants, neglecting coke oven leaks and byproduct production as well as the coking process in non-recovery coking plants; therefore, the existing source profiles may not accurately reflect VOCs emissions from different coking segments. As such, it is crucial to determine localized VOCs source profiles and emission factors based on different emission segments within different types of coking plants in China, specifically, mechanical and nonrecovery coking plants.

Two typical coking plants, a non-recovery coking plant and a mechanical coking plant, were selected to obtain the source profiles of VOCs from different emission segments. The coking plants locate in Shanxi Province, a centralized coking plant area in China. Designed annual yield of coke of non-recovery and mechanical coking plant is 0.5 million tons and 1.6 million tons with the operating load is  $75\%$  and  $75\%$  during the sampling time, respectively. The height of the coke oven in the non-recovery and mechanical coking plant is  $2.1\mathrm{m}$  and  $4.3\mathrm{m}$ , respectively. Detailed information of the coking plants is listed in Table 1. It is important to note the cleaned coal for coking is put into the furnace directly through a closed cleaned coal conveyance corridor in non-recovery coking plant. As the predominantly emitted pathway of pollutants in non-recovery coking plant, stack flue gas is the target in this study. While coke oven leakage is ignored due to the negative pressure conditions of the coke oven during the normal operation. Like most mechanical coking plants, metallurgical coke is produced in coke oven under the positive pressure condition and chemical products, including tar, benzene, and ammonium sulfate, come from coal gas recovery in this studied mechanical coking plant. VOCs can leak from coke oven and pipeline in these processes. Thus, the stack flue gas, oven leaks, and byproduct plant leaks are all investigated in the mechanical coking plant in this study. Samples were collected from the stacks after desulfurization and deducting.

was collected after the byproducts burning and heat recovering in non-recovery coking plant, while different, it was gathered after byproducts recovery in mechanical coking plant. The samples were collected in Tedlar bags (with a volume of 2 L), which are made of Teflon and covered in black plastic. During sampling, the sampling bags were connected to a stainless-steel tube, whose length was enough sufficient to reach into the stack through the sampling holes, and a sampling pump (LP-4™; A.P. Buck Inc., USA), in which all interfaces were sealed. Samples were pumped into the sampling bags at the flow rate of  $1.0 \, \text{L} \cdot \text{min}^{-1}$  for a sampling duration of 2 min. To sample fugitive emissions, i.e., leaks, stainless-steel SUMMA canisters (1.0 L Siliconite® Canisters; Entech Instruments Inc., CA, USA), pre-cleaned with high-purity nitrogen and evacuated to vacuum then equipped with a restricted sampler (39-RS-0; Entech Instruments Inc., CA, USA) were used to collect the instantaneous samples for a sampling duration of 5 min. The sampling sites (Sites C-D) were located at the top of the coke oven according to the State Standard of the People's Republic of China (GB 16171-2012) and approximately 20 m downwind of the byproduct production workshop area, respectively. Parallel experiments were conducted whereby samples were collected from the same sampling point at least three times to ensure that the results were representative.

Emission factors are used as essential data for building the emission inventories and for environmental management. Production engineering, equipment, fuel sources, combustion conditions, treatments, etc. are all factors influencing VOCs source emissions. The VOCs EFs for SFN and SFM were  $96\mathrm{g}\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke and  $0.4\mathrm{g}\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke, respectively (Fig. 4), indicating that the emission intensity from the stack in a non-recovery coking plant is far greater than that in a mechanical coking plant. As the coke oven gas produced by the coking procedure in a non-recovery coking plant operates under negative pressure, the atmospheric pollutants are predominantly discharged through the stack to the atmosphere. However, the EFs of coke production from the mechanical coking plant only involved estimation of the VOCs emissions from the stack flue gas and neglected fugitive emissions such as coke oven and byproduct plant leaks, which partly explains the small EFs from the stack in the mechanical coking plant. As shown in Fig. 4, the EFs of alkenes and aromatics in SFN and SFM were  $67.15\mathrm{g}\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$  and  $0.29\mathrm{g}\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , respectively, representing the groups with highest emission intensity. The major species with high emission intensity in SFN were ethylene, 1-butene, benzene, and acetylene, with EFs of  $40.58\mathrm{g}\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ ,  $25.26\mathrm{g}\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ ,  $11.71\mathrm{g}\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , and  $9.89\mathrm{g}\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , respectively. For SFM, the major species were styrene, benzene, and ethylene, with EFs of  $0.16\mathrm{g}\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ ,  $0.08\mathrm{g}\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , and  $0.04\mathrm{g}\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , respectively. The results of this study indicate that phasing out non-recovery coking plants in China and strongly promoting mechanical coking plants instead would help reduce VOCs emissions, especially alkene emissions, which is significant for improving the ambient air quality.

A significant difference was observed in the EFs of the mechanical coking plant analyzed in this study and those reported in previous studies around the world, such as  $68\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke in USA (U.S. EPA, 2008),  $41\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke in UK (NAEI, 2018), and  $47\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  coal in Denmark (EMEP, 2016). Although the EFs in this study might be lower than the actual emissions because of the limited sampling set, VOCs emissions from the stack in a mechanical coking plant may be overestimated using previous emission factors. Many factors can contribute to this discrepancy, including coal quality, coking process, flue gas treatments, etc. The conversion efficiency from coal to coke varies with the coal quality in different countries, which would lead to the change of VOCs emissions from coking production in different countries. In addition, the emissions of VOCs decreased with the upgrading of coking process and the terminal control measures of flue gas implementing. That may also be the reasons of VOCs emission factors from stack in the studied mechanical coking plant is lower than that in the others results. In terms of the EFs from

We measured the VOCs emitted from various segments of coke production at a non-recovery plant and a mechanical coking plant in China and then analyzed the source profiles, emission factors, and chemical reactivity. content.title: Figure 9. Speciated NMVOC emissions for the year 2010 by chemical group and by Asian regions. Alkanes: ethane, propane, butanes, pentanes, hexanes, higher alkanes and their isomers. Alkenes: ethane, propene, isoprene, terpenes, higher alkenes and their isomers. Alkynes: ethyne and other alkynes. Aromatics: benzene, toluene, xylene, trimethylbenzene, other aromatics and their isomers. OVOCs: aldehydes (formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, and higher aldehydes), ketones (acetone and higher ketones), alcohols (methanol, ethanol, and higher alcohols), ethers, and acids. "Others": halogenated hydrocarbons, unidentified species, etc.
content.summary: Figure 9 displays NMVOC emissions by chemical group and region in Asia for 2010, highlighting contributions from alkanes, alkenes, and OVOCs.
content.insight: The figure illustrates that alkanes and alkenes were primary contributors to 2010 Asian NMVOC emissions, with regional variations and significant industrial sector impact.

metadata.title: Figure 9. Speciated NMVOC emissions for the year 2010 by chemical group and by Asian regions. Alkanes: ethane, propane, butanes, pentanes, hexanes, higher alkanes and their isomers. Alkenes: ethane, propene, isoprene, terpenes, higher alkenes and their isomers. Alkynes: ethyne and other alkynes. Aromatics: benzene, toluene, xylene, trimethylbenzene, other aromatics and their isomers. references: Figure 9 presents 2010 Asian NMVOC emissions of different chemical groups by region and by sector. Similar to Asian emissions estimated in previous work (Klimont et al., 2002; Li et al., 2014), alkanes and alkenes are the largest contributors to the total Asian NMVOC emissions in 2010 (27 and  $26\%$  of the total respectively), followed by oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs;  $20\%$ ), aromatics  $(17\%)$ , and alkynes  $(7\%)$ . Regionally, shares of alkanes and aromatics are higher in China, Other East Asia, Central Asia, and Russia Asia than other regions, due to large contributions from the industrial sector. Shares of alkynes in Central Asia and Russia Asia are significantly lower than other regions due to a low contribution from biofuel emissions. Sectoral contribution of emissions varies significantly by different chemical groups. Over Asia, the industrial sector is the major source of emissions of alkanes and aromatics. Alkanes emissions from industrial sector are mainly contributed by gas production and distribution  $(19.8\%$  of total industrial emissions), coal combustion  $(17.1\%)$ , and oil

content.insight: The figure demonstrates significant differences in VOC emission profiles across coking plant segments. Aromatics are prevalent in SFM, OLM, and BLM, while alkenes dominate SFN, impacting environmental and health risk assessments.
